title: Isaiah 14:24
content_html: I have planned … I have purposed: No nation can stop or resist God's plans to judge Assyria or his plans overall. The prophets understood and shared God's plan so his people could respond properly.

title: Isaiah 14:24–27
content_html: This prophecy continues to declare judgment on Assyria (see Isaiah 10). Placing this prophecy after the judgment against Babylon suggests a close connection between Assyria and Babylon.

title: Isaiah 14:25
content_html: This prophecy told what would happen when Sennacherib attacked Hezekiah some years later in 701 BC (see Isaiah 36–37).

title: Isaiah 14:26
content_html: The Lord’s power over Assyria is just one example of his sovereignty over the whole earth.

title: Isaiah 14:29
content_html:
• The king who attacked Philistia was probably Ahaz (Isaiah 14:28).

  • a viper: This probably refers to one of the later Assyrian kings, either Sennacherib (701 BC), Esarhaddon (680 BC), or Ashurbanipal (668 BC).

title: Isaiah 14:31
content_html: The advancing army, probably Assyria, would stir up clouds of dust like smoke that were ominously visible in the north.

title: Isaiah 14:32
content_html: The LORD has founded Zion: God was fully willing and capable of defending Judah, and he did not want the kingdom to make alliances with nations such as the Philistines to protect themselves from the invading Assyrians.

title: Isaiah 15:1
content_html:
• Moab was located east of the Dead Sea. It was an enemy of Judah.

  • in a night!: The destruction would be quick and final.

title: Isaiah 15:1–16.14
content_html: This message of judgment and sorrow was about Moab's fall. The many references to Moabite places show that Isaiah knew Moab well.

title: Isaiah 15:2
content_html: In the ancient Near East, people shaved or cut off their beards to show deep sorrow (see Jeremiah 48:37).

title: Isaiah 15:4
content_html:
• Heshbon was once the capital of Sihon (Numbers 21:23–26).

  • Elealeh was near Heshbon.

  • Jahaz was located between Heshbon and the Arnon River (see Jeremiah 48:34).

title: Isaiah 15:5
content_html:
• My heart cries out over Moab: The prophet joined the mourning to show how much Moab suffered.

  • Zoar was in the southeastern part of the Dead Sea (Genesis 14:2; 19:23).

title: Isaiah 15:6
content_html:
• The exact location of the waters of Nimrim is unknown (see also Jeremiah 48:34).

  • dried up … withered … the vegetation: For more on natural resources drying up as a form of judgment, see study note on Isaiah 24:4.

title: Isaiah 15:7–8
content_html: The exact locations of the Brook of the Willows, Eglaim, and Beer-elim are unknown.

title: Isaiah 15:9
content_html:
• Dimon is a play on the Hebrew word for blood (dam).

  • blood: Many people from Moab had already died, but their punishment had just started.

title: Isaiah 16:1
content_html:
• Moab was famous for its sheep (see 2 Kings 3:4). The Moabites sent lambs as tribute to Judah, acknowledging Judah's control over Moab.

  • Sela (the cliff), a mysterious and distant location, was likely where the Moabites fled through the desert.

title: Isaiah 16:1–4
content_html: Moab is making a request to Judah for asylum (protection in another country).

title: Isaiah 16:3–4
content_html: do not betray … be a refuge: Moab's situation would become so desperate that they would ask for refuge in other countries.

title: Isaiah 16:4–5
content_html:
• This prophecy of salvation spoke about the future of David’s royal line. It promised hope for the dynasty of David.

  • Acts of oppression and destruction would come to an end. This would happen when the hostility of Moab stopped (see 2 Kings 13:20).

title: Isaiah 16:5
content_html: God would establish one of David’s descendants as king (see also Isaiah 9:7; 11:1–5, 10–12).

title: Isaiah 16:6
content_html: The Moabites were too proud, angry, and boastful to humble themselves before God.

title: Isaiah 16:6–11
content_html: The response to Moab’s request for asylum (Isaiah 16:1–4).

title: Isaiah 16:8
content_html:
• The vineyards at Sibmah were near the Moabite city of Heshbon.

  • Moab was like a lush vine spreading far and wide, but Israel's beautiful vine reached the ends of the earth (Isaiah 27:6).

  • The location of Jazer is unknown.

title: Isaiah 16:12
content_html:
• The hilltops were sites for pagan shrines (see Isaiah 15:2).

  • No one will be able to save them, including the idols the Moabites called on for protection (see Isaiah 40:18–19; 44:18–19).

title: Isaiah 16:14
content_html:
• In three years, as a hired worker counts the years (see also Isaiah 21:16): A man who sold himself into servitude would calculate the remaining time down to the last day. This verse refers to unknown events.

  • The glory of Moab will be ended, probably because of the Assyrian conquest of Moab (late 700s BC).

  • those who are left will be few and feeble: Though reduced by war, the Moabites still existed during Nehemiah’s time (Nehemiah 13:23).

title: Isaiah 17:1
content_html: Damascus, the leader of the alliance between Syria and Israel against Judah, fell when the Assyrian king Tiglath-pileser III invaded in 732 BC.

title: Isaiah 17:1–20.6
content_html: This section includes predictions of judgment against Damascus, Israel, Ethiopia, and Egypt. The scene shifts from the crisis in 734 BC (the alliance of Syria and Israel; see study note on Isaiah 7:1–25) to the Assyrian crisis in 701 BC (see Isaiah 36–37).

title: Isaiah 17:2
content_html:
• The towns of Aroer were east of the Dead Sea (see 2 Kings 10:32–33).

  • they will be left to the flocks: The city would serve no other purpose.

title: Isaiah 17:3
content_html: The strategic fortified towns of Israel were the pride of Israel, but they could not resist God’s purposes.

title: Isaiah 17:4
content_html:
• the splendor of Jacob will fade: God will judge all human pride.

  • Israel, once strong, will become weak. The nation will waste away like a sick person.

title: Isaiah 17:4–11
content_html: The vision shifts from Damascus (Isaiah 17:1–3) to Israel's northern kingdom. Damascus and Israel were allies against Judah, but Assyria later destroyed both.

title: Isaiah 17:5
content_html: The Valley of Rephaim was a fertile area in Judah. However, it appeared barren after each harvest.

title: Isaiah 17:6
content_html: Although the God of Israel judged the wickedness of Israel, he was still its God.

title: Isaiah 17:7
content_html:
• The people mentioned here are either Israelites or all of humanity.

  • look … turn their eyes: They will once more trust in the Lord.

title: Isaiah 17:8
content_html: Canaanites and then Israelites worshiped the fertility goddess Asherah in wooded areas or, in the absence of wooded areas, by using Asherah poles (see Isaiah 27:9; 65:3; Micah 5:14).

title: Isaiah 17:9
content_html: The Hivites and Amorites were part of the original people living in Canaan. The Israelites were to destroy them when they conquered the land (see Deuteronomy 20:17).

title: Isaiah 17:10
content_html:
• The Israelites combined their faith in the Lord with Baal worship. They turned away from the Lord, who is the only God and will not share devotion with idols.

  • The term Rock refers to the Lord's ability to provide refuge (see Isaiah 26:4; 30:29; Psalm 18:2, 46).

title: Isaiah 17:12
content_html: At times, the sea represents opposition to God (see Job 7:12; 26:12; Psalms 46:3; 93:3).

title: Isaiah 17:12–14
content_html: God was present with Judah during the crisis.

title: Isaiah 17:13
content_html: He rebukes them: The Lord rules over the nations, just as he rules over the sea (Psalm 65:7).

title: Isaiah 17:14
content_html:
In the evening … Before morning: Trouble may come at night, but it will not last long.

Even in frightening situations, Israel can trust the Lord. He is able to rescue his people quickly (see Isaiah 37:36–38).

title: Isaiah 18:1
content_html:
Ethiopia was located to the south of Egypt. It marked the southern border of Egypt’s kingdom.

At that time, Egypt was ruled by an Ethiopian dynasty (a family line of kings). One of its rulers was Piankhi, also known as Piye.

title: Isaiah 18:3
content_html: Both the banner and ram's horn were used to organize and send signals to armies during battles (see Judges 3:27; 6:34; 2 Samuel 2:28).

title: Isaiah 18:4
content_html: The Lord is untroubled by human activities. He watches quietly from his dwelling place and acts when he chooses (see Psalm 2:1–4).

title: Isaiah 18:5
content_html: the flower becomes a ripening grape: Ethiopia had made every effort to form alliances to protect itself against Assyria, but its alliance with Judah would not help.

title: Isaiah 18:7
content_html: Conquered or weaker nations often had to pay tribute to a dominant king in the region (see Isiah 16:1; 2 Kings 3:4–5). The Ethiopians brought gifts to honor the Lord for defeating the Assyrians (see also 2 Chronicles 32:23).

title: Isaiah 19:1
content_html:
• Riding on a swift cloud shows God's control and power over creation (Psalm 104:3). He is coming to judge Judah's enemies (see Deuteronomy 33:26; Psalms 18:9–10; 68:4; 97:2–3).

  • The idols of Egypt tremble because they are weak and powerless, unable to help (see Isaiah 40:18; 45:16; 46:1).

title: Isaiah 19:1–10
content_html: Egypt relied heavily on the Nile for food, fish, and industry. When the Lord struck the Nile, Egypt would face severe difficulties, and its gods would not be able to help.

title: Isaiah 19:1–20.6
content_html: See also Jeremiah 46; Ezekiel 29–32.

title: Isaiah 19:2
content_html: Egypt experienced many civil wars that damaged the country. The nation's fragile unity was falling apart.

title: Isaiah 19:3
content_html: People called upon idols, spirits, mediums, and those who consult the spirits of the dead to try to prevent destruction (see Isaiah 8:19–22; 47:12–13).

title: Isaiah 19:4
content_html: harsh master: In 671 BC, the Assyrian king Esarhaddon invaded Egypt and destroyed Memphis, the royal city. In 663 BC, Ashurbanipal attacked and destroyed Thebes (see Nahum 3:8–10).

title: Isaiah 19:5
content_html: The Nile River was essential for Egypt's survival. Any change in its flooding pattern impacted the entire country. In ancient Egypt, people saw the Nile as a god. However, the true God could easily dry it up.

title: Isaiah 19:9
content_html: The workers in flax … and the weavers of fine linen will turn pale: The drought destroyed the flax crops. As a result, the linen industry could not continue.

title: Isaiah 19:11
content_html:
• Zoan was the capital of Egypt, located in the Nile delta (see Numbers 13:22).

  • princes … wise counselors: The king relied on the strategies and advice of wise men (Isaiah 19:12; see Genesis 41:39–40).

  • The officials of Zoan were famous for their wise ancestors, and Egypt often boasted about them.

title: Isaiah 19:11–15
content_html: The prophet mocked the leaders and wise men of Egypt, who could not prevent the disaster.

title: Isaiah 19:12
content_html: Where are your wise men now?: Compare 1 Corinthians 1:20.

title: Isaiah 19:13
content_html: Memphis was an ancient capital in Egypt located south of the Nile Delta. It was destroyed by Esarhaddon in 671 BC.

title: Isaiah 19:15
content_html:
• There is nothing Egypt can do: No leader, idol, or human wisdom can stop God’s judgment.

  • The head and the tail represented all of Egypt's leaders, from the most important to the least important (see Isaiah 9:14–15).

title: Isaiah 19:16
content_html: The uplifted hand (literally waving the hand) was a threatening gesture.

title: Isaiah 19:18
content_html:
• five cities in the land of Egypt: This likely means "a number of cities" (see also Isaiah 17:6; 30:17).

  • Jews living in Egypt after Jerusalem's destruction spoke Hebrew, referred to here as the language of Canaan.

  • Heliopolis was dedicated to Re, the sun god. When Judah resisted the Lord, Isaiah imagined the Egyptians following the Lord.

title: Isaiah 19:19
content_html: This altar or monument might refer to the temple built by Jews at Elephantine in Egypt around 500 BC. It could also refer to another Jewish place of worship or a memorial the Egyptians made to honor the Lord.

title: Isaiah 19:19–25
content_html: The new Egypt would receive a place in God’s purposes together with Assyria and Israel.

title: Isaiah 19:20
content_html:
• The altar (Isaiah 19:19) would be a sign and a witness of the Egyptians’ religious connection with Israel (see 19:24–25).

  • God answers the prayers of those who ask the Lord for help.

  • The Lord is a savior; he would protect the Egyptians just as he protected Jerusalem from the Assyrians.

title: Isaiah 19:22
content_html:
• strike Egypt: The Hebrew verb here is the same as the one used for the tenth plague (Exodus 12:13, 23, 27), reminding us of God's actions during the exodus.

  • The Lord, who sends a plague, can also heal.

  • He will hear their prayers and heal them: This expresses a covenantal relationship between the Lord and Egypt (see Isaiah 65:24).

title: Isaiah 19:23
content_html:
• Traditionally, Egypt and Assyria were enemies of each other and of Israel. They were deeply involved in idol worship, but they would abandon their hostility and idols to worship the true God.

  • In the rugged terrain of the ancient Near East, a highway allowed for safe and easy travel and trade (see Isaiah 11:16; 35:8; 40:3; 62:10).

title: Isaiah 19:24
content_html: Israel will be a blessing, fulfilling God's promise to Abraham (Genesis 12:3).

title: Isaiah 19:25
content_html: Two titles usually meant for Israel, My people and My handiwork (see Isaiah 29:22; 32:18), are used here for foreign nations. The Lord promised to consider those from other nations who turn to him as his special possession (Isaiah 63:17).

title: Isaiah 20:1
content_html:
• Sargon of Assyria is Sargon II, who ruled from 721 to 705 BC.

  • Ashdod was one of the five Philistine cities that rebelled against Assyria in 713 BC.

title: Isaiah 20:1–5
content_html: Ashdod’s reliance on Egypt and Ethiopia was useless. As a complement to his words, Isaiah walked about exposed and barefoot, a symbolic action for what God would do.

title: Isaiah 20:2
content_html:
• Prophets often wore sackcloth or other rough clothing to show mourning for the people's sins (see 2 Kings 1:8; Mark 1:6).

  • naked: This means exposed in some way, possibly even completely nude.

title: Isaiah 20:3–4
content_html: This symbolic event happened about three years before Ashdod fell (see Isaiah 20:2). Through this event, Isaiah served as an example for the people (see Isaiah 8:18).

title: Isaiah 20:5–6
content_html: Philistia’s downfall was a warning to the people of Judah, who also relied on Egypt and other alliances rather than on God.

title: Isaiah 21:1
content_html:
• This is the burden against the Desert by the Sea: This description of Babylon might ironically suggest that the land was physically lush but spiritually empty.

  • from the desert, from a land of terror: (compare Deuteronomy 8:15).

title: Isaiah 21:2
content_html:
• A dire vision is declared to me: The prophet makes Babylon's experience personal, which strengthens his poetry. The concise language and repeated sounds, like The traitor still betrays (Hebrew habboged boged) and the destroyer still destroys (Hebrew hashoded shoded; also in Isaiah 33:1), add to this effect.

  • The Elamites lived northeast of Babylon. They, along with the Medes, were major rivals of the Babylonians.

  • I will put an end to all her groaning: Babylon caused suffering by attacking and enslaving many nations. The Elamites and Medes likely helped overthrow Babylon in 539 BC (Isaiah 21:9).

title: Isaiah 21:3
content_html: I am bewildered to hear, I am dismayed to see: Through his vision, Isaiah felt the fear of the Babylonian people (see also Daniel 8:27; 10:16–17).

title: Isaiah 21:7
content_html: The riders were messengers who reported what had taken place on the battlefield.

title: Isaiah 21:9
content_html:
• fallen is Babylon!: Assyrian King Sennacherib destroyed Babylon in 689 BC. The final fall of Babylon occurred in 539 BC.

    In Scripture, Babylon symbolizes all ungodly power structures, including nations and kingdoms that do not follow God and his word. The apostle John offered hope with his prophecy that all Babylons will fall (Revelation 14:8; 18:2).

  • All the images of her gods lie shattered on the ground!: False gods could not save the Babylonians (see Isaiah 46:1–2).

title: Isaiah 21:10
content_html:
• O my people: The focus returned to the people of Judah.

  • The Babylonians would oppress (thresh and winnow) Judah, but Babylon would also fall.

  • I tell you what I have heard: The prophet faithfully reported what the Lord revealed to him.

title: Isaiah 21:11–12
content_html:
• Seir: This refers to the mountainous region of Edom.

  • Watchman, what is left of the night: Edom's suffering would last a long time. The Assyrians controlled the ancient Near East during the 700s and 600s BC.

title: Isaiah 21:11–17
content_html: These two prophecies are about Edom and Arabia, located between Babylon and Israel. These regions suffered under Assyrian control. Like Judah, they would not find peace under Babylon's harsh rule.

title: Isaiah 21:12
content_html: Morning is coming, but it will start another difficult time. The Babylonians are approaching, so night will soon return.

title: Isaiah 21:13
content_html:
• Dedan was a tribe in Arabia (see Ezekiel 27:20; 38:13).

  • Caravans (groups of traders traveling together) would hide in the deserts of Arabia to escape attack. The Assyrians and Babylonians often attacked and troubled the Arabian tribes (see Isaiah 21:1–10; Jeremiah 49:28–29).

title: Isaiah 21:13–17
content_html: Compare Jeremiah 49:28–33.

title: Isaiah 21:14
content_html: Tema was an oasis in Arabia on a main trade route from Babylon.

title: Isaiah 21:15
content_html: Assyrian weapons of war, such as the drawn sword and the bent bow, were highly advanced compared to the simple weapons used by desert peoples.

title: Isaiah 21:16
content_html: Kedar was a wealthy Arabian tribe with many possessions (see Isaiah 60:7; Ezekiel 27:21).

title: Isaiah 21:17
content_html: The remaining archers … will be few: Compare Isaiah 10:22; 14:30; 16:14; 24:6, 13; 37:32.

title: Isaiah 22:1
content_html: This is the burden against the Valley of Vision: The meaning of the Hebrew phrase is unclear. If it refers to Jerusalem (see Isaiah 22:5), it is ironic. Jerusalem is on a hill, and people did not usually think visions came from a valley. Similarly, Jerusalem was proud of its supposed religious vision, but its people were spiritually blind (see Isaiah 6:10; 42:18).

title: Isaiah 22:1–4
content_html: The prophet scolds the people for celebrating the Assyrian retreat in 701 BC. They did not predict that the Babylonian army would later, in 586 BC, finish what the Assyrians could not: the destruction of Jerusalem.

title: Isaiah 22:1–25
content_html: This message addresses the Assyrian siege of Jerusalem in 701 BC (see Isaiah 36–37; 2 Kings 18:17–19:37). It may also refer to the people's preparation for the later Babylonian siege from 588 to 586 BC.

title: Isaiah 22:2
content_html:
• The city celebrated with feasting and drinking (Isaiah 22:13; see 23:7; 32:13), likely because the Assyrians retreated. Now, a new threat approaches: the Babylonian siege in 586 BC.

  • The people did not die in battle but from famine and disease. The siege cut them off from food and caused a sanitation and health crisis.

title: Isaiah 22:3
content_html: Leaders might try to flee, but they would not escape (see 2 Kings 25:4–6).

title: Isaiah 22:4
content_html: let me weep bitterly!: Isaiah grieved for the victims and the destruction of his people (see Isaiah 6:13).

title: Isaiah 22:5
content_html: The invading Babylonians broke Jerusalem's walls using battering rams (2 Kings 25:4).

title: Isaiah 22:5–8
content_html: The defeat of Judah would show what the future day of the Lord will be like (see study note on Isaiah 2:5–22). Jerusalem would have no power to save itself in that time of disaster.

title: Isaiah 22:6
content_html:
• Elam: Elam was east of Babylon and later helped defeat Babylon (see Isaiah 21:2; also Jeremiah 49:34–39).

  • The exact location of Kir is unknown (see also Amos 1:5).

title: Isaiah 22:7
content_html: Your choicest valleys: This refers to the Kidron and Hinnom valleys, which were located to the east and south of Jerusalem.

title: Isaiah 22:8
content_html: to the weapons: This large and impressive building stored weapons. It also stored valuable items (see Isaiah 39:2; 1 Kings 7:2–6).

title: Isaiah 22:8–11
content_html:
The people of Jerusalem prepared for war. They counted their weapons, inspected the city walls, and made sure they had enough water.

However, they did not think about God’s plans (see Isaiah 13:1–14:23). They focused on their own efforts instead of seeking the Lord.

King Hezekiah played an important role in these preparations. However, the verbs in this passage are plural. Isaiah was speaking to the people as a group, not only to Hezekiah. This is different from the way Isaiah addressed Hezekiah’s father, Ahaz (Isaiah 7:1–4).

title: Isaiah 22:9
content_html:
• the City of David: Calling Jerusalem the city of David brings to mind Israel’s greatest king and God's blessing on him and the nation (see also Isaiah 60:14).

  • You collected water from the lower pool: Hezekiah worked hard to ensure a steady water supply (see Isaiah 22:11; 2 Kings 20:20).

title: Isaiah 22:12–14
content_html: Judah’s feasting and disobedience resulted in a prophecy of judgment.

title: Isaiah 22:14
content_html:
this sin of yours will never be atoned for: The people of Judah kept adding sin to sin. Because of this, God would rightly judge and condemn them.

This warning looked ahead to the exile. The exile happened in 586 BC. However, Isaiah was already warning about it as early as 701 BC.

title: Isaiah 22:15
content_html:
• Shebna's actions show that the people do not trust God to save Jerusalem from the Assyrian attack.

  • A palace administrator had the top position in the royal court (see Isaiah 22:21–22).

title: Isaiah 22:15–25
content_html:
Shebna and Eliakim were two royal officials in King Hezekiah’s court (see 2 Kings 18:18; 19:2). Shebna believed the Assyrians would surely kill him. So he planned for a well-decorated burial place (Isaiah 22:16).

Although the rebuke of Shebna happened before Assyria’s siege of Jerusalem in 701 BC (see Isaiah 22:19–21; 36:3), it relates well to the warnings in 22:1–13 about the later Babylonian siege.

title: Isaiah 22:16
content_html: Shebna was building an impressive tomb for himself. It was meant to be a monument. This large and costly burial place was designed to last for many generations.

title: Isaiah 22:18
content_html:
• sling you into a wide land: Shebna would not find a resting place or have a memorial (compare Isaiah 14:11–20).

  • your glorious chariots: In his high position, Shebna lived in luxury.

title: Isaiah 22:19–20
content_html: remove you from office: Shebna (Isaiah 22:15) was demoted to court secretary by the time of Jerusalem’s siege. Eliakim (Isaiah 22:20) was the palace administrator (36:3). Eliakim was a true servant of the Lord.

title: Isaiah 22:21
content_html: A leader is like a father to the people when he truly cares for them and addresses their needs. In contrast, Shebna was concerned only for himself.

title: Isaiah 22:22
content_html:
• The key to the house of David represents a high position of honor in the royal court (see also Revelation 3:7).

  • What he opens… what he shuts: The highest-ranking officer has the sole authority to grant access to the king (compare Matthew 16:19).

title: Isaiah 22:25
content_html: the peg … will give way: Even righteous Eliakim could not save Judah or David's family line from God's judgment. The people of Judah would go into exile over a hundred years later (Isaiah 39:6–7).

title: Isaiah 23:1
content_html:
• The ships of Tarshish traveled the sea between the Phoenician coast and the port of Tarshish in Spain (see Isaiah 2:16; 23:6).

  • Word has reached them from the land of Cyprus: Cyprus was the last stop for sailors returning from Spain before their final journey to Tyre.

title: Isaiah 23:1–18
content_html:
Tyre was a wealthy seaport northwest of Israel. The connection between Judah and Tyre dates back to the time of David and Solomon (1 Kings 5:8–9).

Tyre was near large forests and the Mediterranean Sea. It was a major trade center in the ancient Near East. Its ships were famous, and its colonies gave it great prestige and wealth.

The city faced repeated attacks by the Assyrians, Babylonians, and Macedonians, and Alexander the Great conquered it in 332 BC. Isaiah not only predicted Tyre's downfall but also warned those whose wealth and security lead to pride instead of reliance on God (see Isaiah 2:12–16; Matthew 11:21).

title: Isaiah 23:2
content_html:
• Sidon was a Phoenician port located to the north of Tyre.

  • Tyre’s commercial network of traders was extensive.

title: Isaiah 23:3
content_html:
• People harvested fish, crops, flax, and linen along the Nile (see Isaiah 19).

  • Tyre was a global marketplace, similar to modern cities like New York, Hong Kong, or London.

title: Isaiah 23:4
content_html:
• Part of the great port city of Tyre was on an island. The island city was a sea fortress, but it fell in 332 BC.

  • I have not been in labor or given birth. I have not raised young men or brought up young women: This metaphor was a judgment on Tyre’s colonial ports around the Mediterranean Sea. In the ancient world, reaching old age without children was considered a terrible fate (see Isaiah 49:21; 54:1).

title: Isaiah 23:5
content_html: Egypt relied on ships from Tyre to export its products (see Isaiah 23:3).

title: Isaiah 23:8
content_html:
• The Lord had brought this disaster on Tyre (Isaiah 23:9).

  • Through trade, Tyre gained wealth for itself and its partners. In this way, Tyre helped create kingdoms.

title: Isaiah 23:10
content_html:
Cultivate your land like the Nile: This difficult phrase means either:

  1. that the sailors returning from Tarshish were invited to loot the ruined Tyre; or

  2. that the people of Tarshish had to return to farming to earn a living, since Tyre was destroyed and trading was no longer possible.

title: Isaiah 23:11
content_html: Phoenicia was the region north of Israel. Tyre and Sidon were its main cities.

title: Isaiah 23:13
content_html: The Assyrians, led by Sennacherib, stripped and looted Babylonia in 689 BC. Eventually, Babylonia rose to become a mighty empire again.

title: Isaiah 23:15–18
content_html: There was hope for the city of Tyre.

title: Isaiah 23:17
content_html: Metaphorically, Tyre was like a prostitute because it was willing to sell anything to anyone without concern (see Revelation 17:2; 18:3, 12–13).

title: Isaiah 23:18
content_html:
• Although Tyre earned money through dishonest trade, the profits will go to the Lord instead of being wasted.

  • The Lord's priests will receive good food and fine clothing. The idea of nations bringing their wealth to the Lord in Jerusalem is further explored in Isaiah 60:5–11; 61:6.

title: Isaiah 24:1
content_html:
• lays waste the earth and leaves it in ruins: There is a play here on the Hebrew words baqaq (lays waste) and balaq (leaves it in ruins). The devastation will start with Judah and spread to the entire world (see 1 Peter 4:17).

  • All the earth will face destruction, including its people and political systems.

title: Isaiah 24:1–23
content_html: Here, the judgments described in Isaiah 13–23 apply to all of creation.

title: Isaiah 24:1–27.13
content_html:
This section is often called the Little Apocalypse. It is given this name because it has similarities to the book of Revelation.

In these chapters, Isaiah moves from his present time into a vision of the future. The language is broad and universal. Because of this, it is difficult to connect the events to one specific historical situation.

These chapters are not meant to provide a detailed timeline of future events. They are not a historical blueprint. Instead, Isaiah uses vivid images to describe a dramatic future world that is both similar to and different from the present.

The vision includes elements of both the old age and the new age. For example, the people of Zion are described as righteous (new), yet they still wait for their full redemption (old). This pattern fits with the New Testament teaching that the future age has already begun but has not yet fully arrived (2 Corinthians 4:4; Galatians 1:4).

Peter wrote that believers were already living in the last days (1 Peter 1:12; 2 Peter 3:3). At the same time, he said that the final day of the Lord is still future (2 Peter 3:10). These passages show that the present age and the future age overlap.

title: Isaiah 24:4
content_html: earth … withers; the world languishes and fades: The prophets often used drought imagery to capture the attention of those who depend on the land (see also Jeremiah 23:10; Joel 1:12; Amos 1:2).

title: Isaiah 24:4–13
content_html: All humanity is guilty (Romans 3:23) and faces condemnation (see Isaiah 59:1–15). Judgment is like a failed grape harvest that stops drinking and feasting.

title: Isaiah 24:5
content_html: Isaiah likely referred to the everlasting covenant that God made with all humanity through Noah (Genesis 9:8–17). The prophet noticed a similarity between the wickedness of his time and that of the world before the flood.

title: Isaiah 24:6
content_html: Sin has such a strong impact that nothing on earth can fully make up for it. Our hope is in God. Through Christ, God has made peace with the world (Colossians 1:19–20).

title: Isaiah 24:7
content_html:
People who once celebrated will now sigh and mourn. They depended on wine for their pleasure and joy.

When the wine is gone, their happiness is gone as well.

title: Isaiah 24:8
content_html: Tambourines and the sweet sounds of the harp represent happy times (see Isaiah 5:12; Revelation 18:22).

title: Isaiah 24:10
content_html:
city of chaos: The normal problems of city life will grow much worse. Disorder will become chaotic and dangerous.

Because of this, people will hide inside their homes. They will lock their doors to stay safe.

title: Isaiah 24:11
content_html: There is no joy left in the land, not because of an official order, but because the people have no reason to celebrate.

title: Isaiah 24:13
content_html:
• like a harvested olive tree: The remnant will include godly people from both Israel (see Isaiah 27:6) and other nations.

  • The theme of the remnant is common in Isaiah (see “The Remnant” Theme Note; see also Isaiah 10:22; 14:30; 16:14; 24:6; 37:32).

title: Isaiah 24:14–16
content_html: Despite the cries and destruction, praise comes from everywhere. Even while feeling the pain of the old world's ruin, the faithful look forward with joy to the new world.

title: Isaiah 24:16
content_html:
• I am wasting away: The prophets knew the emotional pain caused by their people's sins and its consequences (see Isaiah 15:5; 21:4; Jeremiah 15:10; Micah 1:8).

  • Even though parts of the new world have begun to appear, the lies and evil ways of the old world still control people for a time.

title: Isaiah 24:18
content_html:
• No one can avoid the day of the Lord (see study note on Isaiah 2:5–22).

  • the windows of heaven are open: This image is similar to the great flood in Noah's time (see study note on Genesis 7:11–12).

  • the foundations of the earth are shaken: An earthquake often marks a theophany, which is a physical sign of God's presence (see also Isaiah 6:4; 13:13; 19:1).

title: Isaiah 24:21
content_html:
The phrase translated as host of heaven comes from the Hebrew tseba’ hammarom, which means “armed army of the heavens.” This phrase may refer to pagan gods that people believed were represented by the stars.

It may also refer to angels. Some scholars think it could even include demons (Revelation 12:4, 9). The judgment described here will reach every part of creation. It will affect both the earth and the heavens (Isaiah 13:13; 34:5; see Ephesians 6:11–12).

title: Isaiah 24:22
content_html: confined to a dungeon and punished: See also 2 Peter 2:4; Revelation 9:2, 11; 17:8.

title: Isaiah 25:1–5
content_html: Isaiah praises God for protecting those in need (see Isaiah 12:1–6).

title: Isaiah 25:1–12
content_html: The second part of the Little Apocalypse (see study note on Isaiah 24:1–27:13) describes praise for God's judgment (Isaiah 25:1–5, 11–12) and salvation (25:6–9).

title: Isaiah 25:3
content_html: Assyria and Egypt were strong and ruthless nations, but in the future, they will honor God instead of their own power.

title: Isaiah 25:6
content_html: On this mountain: This means the mountain of the Lord (see Isaiah 2:2; 24:23). This refers not to a geographical city but to Zion, the eternal city of God (see Isaiah 1:8, 27).

title: Isaiah 25:6–8
content_html: The banquet is a symbol of God’s providing richly for all those who will receive his gracious invitation (Revelation 3:20–21; 19:9).

title: Isaiah 25:8
content_html:
• He will swallow up death forever: Isaiah hoped in God's power over sin and its harmful effects. In the New Testament, Jesus Christ fulfills this promise (1 Corinthians 15:54).

  • The Lord's presence will provide eternal comfort. He will wipe away all tears (see Revelation 21:4).

title: Isaiah 25:9–12
content_html: The new community of God’s people will respond in joy and faith. When the enemy is defeated, God’s people will be free forever.

title: Isaiah 25:10
content_html:
• on this mountain: This refers to Mount Zion in Jerusalem.

  • Moab (see Isaiah 15:1–16:14) represents the nations facing judgment, those who rely only on themselves (see Isaiah 34:5–17).

  • as straw is trodden: The people would be helpless and dying in very shameful circumstances.

title: Isaiah 25:11
content_html: Isaiah used the image of a swimmer forcibly kept under water to illustrate that Moab’s people are hopelessly doomed and their human pride will be conquered.

title: Isaiah 26:1
content_html:
• The city refers to Zion, the eternal city of God, where the Lord is present to protect and bless his people (see Isaiah 1:8, 27; see also Psalm 46). Zion stands in direct contrast with the "fortified towns" of the nations (Isaiah 25:2; 26:5), which are helpless and reduced to "a heap of rubble."

  • The walls of God's salvation protectively enclose his people. No one can harm them again.

title: Isaiah 26:1–6
content_html: The new community of God’s people is likened to the citizens of a city, securely held together by the Lord. They are righteous, faithful, peaceful, and trusting.

title: Isaiah 26:1–21
content_html: This part of the Little Apocalypse (see study note on Isaiah 24:1–27:13) is a song of praise and a prayer for salvation.

title: Isaiah 26:2
content_html: Open access is given to all who, like God and his Messiah, are righteous and faithful. The citizens of true Zion are committed to the Lord.

title: Isaiah 26:3
content_html: A perfect peace, marked by harmony, quietness, and confidence, is a benefit of the new creation.

title: Isaiah 26:5
content_html: Proud and arrogant people often praise themselves (see Isaiah 2:11–12), but God humbles them.

title: Isaiah 26:6
content_html: Justice finally occurs when the needy walk all over the ruins of the city where the proud have perished.

title: Isaiah 26:7
content_html: God will do what is right for those who follow his path, even if they suffer a bit longer (see Proverbs 24:16). One day, they will find peace and honesty (see Proverbs 2:8–9; 15:19).

title: Isaiah 26:7–21
content_html: The godly pray for the end of oppression and the full establishment of God’s kingdom. Isaiah encouraged the godly to persevere as they wait for their final vindication and the resurrection of the body.

title: Isaiah 26:8
content_html:
• we wait for You (see Isaiah 26:3–4): Waiting means trusting in the Lord and hoping for his redemption.

  • People demonstrate their commitment to God by obeying his laws; just saying it is not enough.

title: Isaiah 26:9
content_html: longs for You … seeks You: Compare Psalm 63:1.

title: Isaiah 26:10
content_html: God has shown kindness to those who do wrong. Even though they deserve judgment (see also Romans 2:4; 2 Peter 3:9), he has been patient and forgiving and has blessed them. However, one day, time will run out.

title: Isaiah 26:11
content_html:
• A raised fist shows anger and a threat of judgment (see also Isaiah 19:16).

  • Fire was often an image for God’s judgment.

title: Isaiah 26:15
content_html: This verse is a confident expression of thanks for God’s blessings. In Isaiah’s time, during King Hezekiah’s prosperous reign, the borders of the nation of Israel were extended, and people gave glory to God for his great deeds.

title: Isaiah 26:16
content_html: While waiting for God's salvation (Isaiah 26:1), the faithful were in distress and longed for justice. They prayed under the weight of God's discipline. During Hezekiah's time, Judah was in distress when Sennacherib attacked and destroyed the entire nation except for Jerusalem.

title: Isaiah 26:17
content_html: The image of a pregnant woman in childbirth shows the nation's pain when Assyrian King Sennacherib attacked in 701 BC (see Isaiah 37:3).

title: Isaiah 26:18
content_html: Only God can give salvation and bring life (see Isaiah 59:9–15).

title: Isaiah 26:19
content_html:
Your dead will live: There is hope for the remnant. Some hold the minimal interpretation that this means the people would be restored to the land after the exile in Babylon (see Ezekiel 37:11–12) or that the Lord would preserve Jerusalem (see Isaiah 29:4, 6–8).

More likely, it refers to the resurrection of the body from the grave (see Isaiah 25:7–8). The Old Testament says little about the resurrection, but this text anticipates more detailed discussion in the New Testament (see 1 Corinthians 15:12–58).

title: Isaiah 26:20
content_html: God will punish the wicked, so the faithful should go home and avoid trouble.

title: Isaiah 26:21
content_html: The wicked hold power now, but their oppressive rule will end on the day of the Lord's wrath. On that day, he will punish them for their sins.

title: Isaiah 27:1
content_html: Israel used the image of Leviathan to represent evil powers that oppose God. Leviathan's death symbolizes the end of evil, Satan, demons, and forces hostile to God (see Isaiah 51:9; Psalms 74:14; 104:7–9, 26; see also the serpent in Genesis 3; Revelation 12; 13; 16:13; 20:2, 10).

title: Isaiah 27:2–6
content_html: In the Song of the Fruitful Vineyard, the vineyard represents God’s people (compare Isaiah 5:1–7).

title: Isaiah 27:3
content_html:
• God calls himself the Lord of the covenant to assure his people that his words and actions are trustworthy (see Exodus 6:2–3). This phrase appears many times in Isaiah.

  • keeper … water … guard: The Lord will give even greater care and protection to his ultimate vineyard than he did to Israel, his first vineyard (see Isaiah 5:1–2).

title: Isaiah 27:5
content_html:
• God wants everyone to turn to him and trust him for help.

  • God offers his people a chance to make peace with him.

title: Isaiah 27:6
content_html:
• Jacob’s descendants will grow, and the remaining people will increase (Isaiah 37:31; see also 5:24; 14:30; for application to the Messiah, see 11:1, 10).

  • fill the whole world: This prophecy goes beyond Israel's return from exile to the second coming of the Lord Jesus. It includes all righteous children of God (Jews and non-Jews) who suffer but stay obedient while waiting for complete redemption (see Isaiah 26:18; John 15:1–8).

title: Isaiah 27:7
content_html: Even though Israel was God's chosen people, they faced punishment like other wicked nations. However, Israel was not entirely destroyed. A small remnant survived.

title: Isaiah 27:7–11
content_html:
Isaiah reminded readers of the reasons for the exile and judgment:

  • Israel’s sinfulness,

  • God’s righteous judgment, and

  • the absence of divine compassion.

title: Isaiah 27:8
content_html:
• The Assyrians forced many people from the northern kingdom of Israel into exile when Samaria fell in 722 BC. The Babylonians exiled many from Judah between 605 and 586 BC.

  • Storms from the east were famous for their destructive effects (see Job 27:21; 38:24; Jeremiah 4:11).

title: Isaiah 27:9
content_html:
• God's justice requires him to punish sin to correct behavior and purify. The exile in Babylon happened to cleanse Israel's wickedness and remove all of Israel's sins (see Romans 11:27).

  • This aimed to end all pagan worship in Israel, including the incense altars and the poles used for worshiping the goddess Asherah.

title: Isaiah 27:10
content_html: Places previously associated with human power and accomplishment would be ruined to the extent that animals would graze in them.

title: Isaiah 27:11
content_html:
The people were living outside God’s covenant care (his special promise relationship with them). They had turned away from him.

This was a foolish choice. They rejected the protection and blessing that came from belonging to God.

title: Isaiah 27:12
content_html:
• God's withholding of mercy from Israel (Isaiah 27:11) will be temporary.

  • from the flowing Euphrates to the Wadi of Egypt: God's remnant will return from Assyria and Egypt to the land God promised to Abraham (Genesis 15:18; see 1 Kings 4:21).

title: Isaiah 27:13
content_html: The great ram’s horn was blown to gather the people. Its loud sound called them together (see Matthew 24:31).

title: Isaiah 28:1
content_html:
• The drunkards of Israel showed pride, spiritual laziness, and disrespect for God and his commands.

  • Unlike Jerusalem, Samaria was in a fertile valley.

title: Isaiah 28:1–29
content_html: The glory of Samaria, capital of the northern kingdom of Israel, is here contrasted with the glory of the Lord. This section represents the first of the six woes (Woe to...).

title: Isaiah 28:1–33.24
content_html: This section is connected by a series of six threats of judgment or woes (compare Isaiah 5:8–23). After the initial address to Samaria in Isaiah 28:1–13, the rest of the section addresses Judah. This is mainly because Judah tends to trust Egypt to save them from Assyria (Isaiah 30:2).

title: Isaiah 28:2
content_html:
• the Lord has one who is: The covenant God will act against his own people, whose behavior had effectively disqualified them as God’s people (see Hosea 1:9).

  • one who is strong and mighty: This refers to the Assyrians, who destroyed Samaria in 722 BC.

title: Isaiah 28:5–6
content_html: In the future, after God’s judgment has purged his people, he will restore a righteous remnant, transforming the desires and character of his people. Samaria will no longer be the source of Israel’s pride. Instead, they will glory in the Lord, their true crown.

title: Isaiah 28:7
content_html:
• The priests and prophets led the people astray.

  • Intoxicating beverages such as wine and alcohol were prohibited for priests while fulfilling their duties (Leviticus 10:9).

title: Isaiah 28:7–13
content_html: The focus shifts back to the unfortunate current situation in Israel (and Judah). The leaders and people will be trapped by their own plans.

title: Isaiah 28:9–10
content_html: The people had become resistant to God's message (1 Corinthians 14:21).

title: Isaiah 28:11
content_html:
• God will communicate through situations like hardship, exile, and death to reach his stubborn people.

  • foreign tongues: In the New Testament, Paul used this text to describe the spiritual gift of tongues as a sign of judgment for unbelievers (see 1 Corinthians 14:21–22).

title: Isaiah 28:12
content_html: If his people had listened, God's revelation would have led them to rest (see Isaiah 48:18–19). However, they did not listen because of their stubborn hearts (Isaiah 65:12).

title: Isaiah 28:13
content_html: line on line, line on line; a little here, a little there: The Hebrew text is hard to understand. The words might be intentional nonsense to illustrate the point that the people were so spiritually blind that God’s clearest revelation was nonsense to them (see Isaiah 6:9–10).

title: Isaiah 28:14–22
content_html: After giving judgment on Samaria (Isaiah 28:1–13), Isaiah now judges Jerusalem. He accuses Judah's leaders of being as blind as those in Israel's northern kingdom.

title: Isaiah 28:15
content_html:
• have made a covenant with death … fashioned an agreement with Sheol: There are two possible interpretations:

    1. The people had pledged themselves to the Canaanite god of death, Mot, for his supposed protection.

    2. The prophet was sarcastic. The people believed their political and economic actions ensured their safety, but they had actually harmed themselves.

  • lies … and falsehood: The leaders did not see themselves as deceptive, but they were.

title: Isaiah 28:16
content_html: The Lord is the dependable cornerstone in Jerusalem, a valuable foundation stone of God's true city. There would be real hope in Jerusalem if the leaders and people turned to Him. By trusting in God, they would be safe during the worst storm or earthquake (see Isaiah 8:14; Psalm 118:22; Matthew 7:24–27; 21:42; see the application to Jesus Christ, Romans 9:33; 10:11; 1 Corinthians 3:11; Ephesians 2:20; 1 Peter 2:4–7).

title: Isaiah 28:17
content_html: The Lord judges people based on their actions, which show if they have true faith (for justice and righteousness, see Isaiah 1:21–23).

title: Isaiah 28:20
content_html: the bed … the blanket: The leaders were not ready for God's judgment.

title: Isaiah 28:21
content_html:
• as at Mount Perazim: See 2 Samuel 5:20.

  • God rescued Israel with a hailstorm at Gibeon (Joshua 10:10–12), but now he would oppose them.

title: Isaiah 28:23
content_html: Listen: he analogy that follows was written as a wisdom poem, with Isaiah calling on his audience to pay careful attention and to discern what is right.

title: Isaiah 28:24–29
content_html: A farmer knows that there are right and wrong ways to do things, but the leaders of Judah were not that intelligent.

title: Isaiah 28:27–28
content_html: The Lord uses different tools, such as a heavy sledge or a light stick, to achieve his goals. There are different levels of judgment.

title: Isaiah 28:28
content_html: it is not endlessly threshed: This process would end. After judgment would come redemption.

title: Isaiah 28:29
content_html: wonderful in counsel (see Isaiah 9:6): God’s people should learn wisdom about God’s ways, as the farmer has done.

title: Isaiah 29:1–2
content_html: Ariel was another name for Mount Zion (Isaiah 29:8). It likely means "altar of God."

title: Isaiah 29:1–8
content_html: During the Assyrian siege, the Lord would fight for Jerusalem. He would eventually make the Assyrians leave the city.

title: Isaiah 29:1–14
content_html: This is the second of the six woes (Woe …).

title: Isaiah 29:3
content_html: The Lord himself would come against Jerusalem, surrounding and attacking it.

title: Isaiah 29:4
content_html: God aimed to destroy the sinful pride of Jerusalem's people. Their voices would rise as if from the grave. Through the Assyrians, God would humble his people, but he would not abandon them.

title: Isaiah 29:6
content_html:
• you will be visited: God will rescue his people.

  • thunder and earthquake … tempest and consuming flame of fire: These events describe a theophany (a visible appearance of God; see study note on Isaiah 5:25).

title: Isaiah 29:7
content_html: The Assyrians suddenly ended the siege, as if in a dream or a vision in the night. God would save Judah and judge Assyria.

title: Isaiah 29:8
content_html: Like a hungry or thirsty person, the Assyrians could taste the victory over Jerusalem. Yet they never became victorious.

title: Isaiah 29:8–12
content_html:
Jerusalem’s blindness kept its people from understanding God’s plan.

Here, blindness does not mean physical blindness. It means spiritual blindness. The people could see with their eyes, but they did not understand what God was doing.

title: Isaiah 29:10
content_html:
• a spirit of deep sleep: God reinforced the people's foolishness as a judgment on them (compare Romans 1:24–32; 11:8). They could not see reality.

  • People sometimes called prophets visionaries. Neither the people nor their prophets understood what God was doing (Isaiah 6:9–10).

title: Isaiah 29:13
content_html:
• These people used religious language in their prayers and conversations (see Matthew 15:8; Mark 7:6–7), but they did not truly honor God.

  • In their hearts, they were not committed to the Lord.

  • In their worship, they followed human rules and regulations instead of God's word.

title: Isaiah 29:14
content_html: Human wisdom and intelligence will disappear (see 1 Corinthians 1:19). The prophet had already spoken about the failure of Egypt’s wise men (Isaiah 19:11–12). Even Judah’s wise men would make mistakes.

title: Isaiah 29:15
content_html:
• Their plans might refer to the advice that royal counselors were giving Hezekiah, who at first attempted to free himself from Assyria by making alliances with Egypt (in 715 or 701 BC).

  • The people secretly planned evil deeds, but God saw everything.

title: Isaiah 29:15–24
content_html: This threat of coming judgment is the third of the six woes (see study note on Isaiah 28:1–33:24). It begins with judgment but moves to a vision of creation being renewed and of the wicked coming to an end.

title: Isaiah 29:16
content_html:
• potter … clay: This picture shows that the Lord has full authority over his creation. A potter has the right to shape clay as he chooses. In the same way, God has the right to guide and rule his people.

    Scripture does not forbid people from asking God honest and difficult questions. However, it does not allow people to resist or reject God’s will (see Isaiah 10:15; 45:9; 64:8; Romans 9:20).

  • He did not make me?: When people speak this way about God, they refuse to admit that God created them. They deny that God is closely involved in every part of their lives.

title: Isaiah 29:17
content_html:
The forests of Lebanon usually picture rich and healthy growth (see Isaiah 2:13; 14:8). They are often a symbol of strength and beauty.

But here, the forests represent destruction and emptiness. Instead of life and growth, they show ruin and loss.

title: Isaiah 29:18
content_html:
The people were deaf and blind in their hearts and spirits. This means they would not listen to God or understand his truth (see Isaiah 6:10; 42:18; 43:8).

Yet God promised that he would renew humanity and all creation. He would bring healing and new life (see Isaiah 35:1–5).

title: Isaiah 29:21
content_html: The false testimony that led to the oppression of the poor through trickery in the courts of Isaiah’s era would end. Because of God’s work in their hearts, the people would turn from their sinful behavior of the past.

title: Isaiah 29:22
content_html:
• Abraham was the father of all Israel (see Genesis 12:1–3; see also Galatians 3:29).

  • Saved from human mistreatment and God's judgment, the people would no longer feel ashamed. Their disgrace came from the failure of what they had trusted (see Psalm 71:1; 1 Peter 2:6).

title: Isaiah 29:22–24
content_html: The prophecy of woe, which began at 29:15, now ends with a prophecy of salvation.

title: Isaiah 30:1
content_html: The people of Judah did not follow God's guidance (compare Isaiah 11:2). They made alliances that God did not approve. The Assyrian attack on Jerusalem was overwhelming. By allying with Egypt, Judah's leaders showed they relied on human help instead of God. Fortunately, Hezekiah later changed his mind (see Isaiah 36–37).

title: Isaiah 30:1–5
content_html: This prophecy warned Judah not to rely on Egypt. It is the fourth of six woes (see study note on Isaiah 28:1–33:24).

title: Isaiah 30:1–33
content_html: After warning Judah of judgment due to their stubbornness and reliance on Egypt (Isaiah 30:1–17), God promised to show compassion to his people and defeat their enemies.

title: Isaiah 30:4
content_html: Zoan: See Isaiah 19:11. The location of Hanes is uncertain.

title: Isaiah 30:6
content_html:
• Caravans traveled from Judah through the Negev and the Sinai Peninsula to Egypt. They avoided the main road along the coast because Assyria controlled it.

    This desert region was dangerous. Lions and poisonous snakes lived there. The journey put people’s lives at risk. Yet they were willing to face this danger to form an alliance that would not help them.

  • they carry their wealth … and their treasures: This may refer to money and valuable goods that Judah sent to Egypt. They likely sent these riches to pay for Egypt’s protection and support.

title: Isaiah 30:6–7
content_html: This taunt was against those who were busy trying to please the Egyptians in order to get Egypt’s military support.

title: Isaiah 30:7
content_html: Rahab Who Sits Still: In ancient mythology, Rahab was an enemy of the good gods and appeared as a fearsome creature, similar to a giant crocodile (see Isaiah 27:1; 51:9; Job 41:1–34). By calling Egypt futile, the prophet meant it would not help Judah.

title: Isaiah 30:8–11
content_html: God instructed the prophet to write down the vision for future generations. The present generation was rebellious. They chose false prophets' fantasies over God's word (see Isaiah 8:16–17).

title: Isaiah 30:11
content_html: Rid us: The people of Judah did not want to be confronted about sin or judgment.

title: Isaiah 30:12
content_html:
The whole society accepted injustice and lies. People encouraged one another to believe things that were not true.

They believed that Egypt would protect them from Assyria, even though that was false. Because many people accepted this idea, it seemed normal and wise to trust Egypt.

It also became accepted to take land from the poor. Leaders treated this oppression as if it were legal and right.

title: Isaiah 30:12–17
content_html: Judgment would suddenly overtake the people of Judah because they were content with their fantasy world, and they refused God’s path to rest and quietness.

title: Isaiah 30:15
content_html:
• The people of Judah needed to turn away from their sinful ways (Isaiah 30:12–14) and return to the Lord to be saved.

  • Trusting in the Lord would bring peace and confidence, unlike their desperate talks with Egypt.

title: Isaiah 30:16
content_html: Judah relied on Egypt for fast horses (see Psalm 33:17), rejecting God's help and risking its downfall.

title: Isaiah 30:17
content_html:
• one … five: This curse is the opposite of God's blessing for obedience (Leviticus 26:8; Deuteronomy 32:30).

  • The Assyrians had taken control of most of the land of Judah. Only Jerusalem remained.

    The city stood alone, like a single pole on top of a hill. It was isolated and surrounded by enemy territory.

title: Isaiah 30:18
content_html:
• A faithful God will restore order to the world by punishing the wicked and saving his people from them (see Isaiah 1:27; Psalm 96:10–12).

  • God blesses those who wait. Faithful people do not rush to solve their own problems. Instead, they rely on his power and goodness.

title: Isaiah 30:18–33
content_html:
This prophecy of salvation gave promises of hope. It helped Israel look away from their present suffering.

Instead, it pointed them toward the glorious future that God had prepared for his people.

title: Isaiah 30:19
content_html:
• This was likely a challenge and promise to Hezekiah during the Assyrian siege of Jerusalem (see Isaiah 36–37). If he trusted in God, his people would not weep anymore.

  • The Lord cares for his people's needs. When they ask for something, he will surely respond.

title: Isaiah 30:20
content_html: adversity and the water of affliction: A city under enemy siege for a long time would eventually face starvation and disease. God did not intend for this disaster to destroy the people of Jerusalem. Instead, it was meant to teach them.

title: Isaiah 30:21
content_html: The people’s own ears will hear and follow God’s instructions (see Isaiah 6:9–10; 29:18; 35:5).

title: Isaiah 30:22
content_html: One aspect of Hezekiah’s reforms was to remove silver idols and other idolatry of Ahaz’s era and to call the people back to the worship of the God of Israel (2 Kings 18:4–5; 2 Chronicles 29).

title: Isaiah 30:23–24
content_html: The people of Judah would again enjoy covenant blessings rather than curses (see Leviticus 26:4).

title: Isaiah 30:26
content_html: The Lord who struck his people would also heal them and cure the wounds he gave them.

title: Isaiah 30:27–33
content_html: In this prophecy of judgment against the nations, Isaiah foretold that Assyria’s power would be broken by God’s strong arm, not by the Egyptians.

title: Isaiah 30:28
content_html: like a rushing torrent that rises to the neck: Assyria’s destruction would be as devastating as their invasion of Judah (see Isaiah 8:8).

title: Isaiah 30:30
content_html:
• His mighty arm: This phrase is a picture of great strength. It reminds readers of how God defeated the Egyptians during the exodus from Egypt (Isaiah 63:12; see Exodus 15:6).

  • consuming fire … hailstones: Fire and hail often appear when God shows himself in power. These signs are connected with a theophany (a visible appearance of God). They show that God is acting in judgment and authority (see study note on Isaiah 5:25).

title: Isaiah 30:31
content_html: scepter: The Lord, the true King, would be victorious over the threatening Assyrian king.

title: Isaiah 30:32
content_html: tambourines and lyres: Instruments once silenced in judgment (Isaiah 24:8) would be used again by God's people. This time, they would celebrate the Lord's goodness instead of becoming spiritually complacent (see Isaiah 5:12).

title: Isaiah 30:33
content_html: Topheth was a pagan altar in the Valley of Ben-hinnom, outside Jerusalem. At this place, some people burned their children as sacrifices to the Ammonite god Molech. This was part of false worship (2 Kings 23:9–10).

title: Isaiah 31:1
content_html:
• Woe: This is the fifth of the six woes (see study note on Isaiah 28:1–33:24).

  • seek the LORD: This suggests seeking God's guidance with a worshipful attitude (see Deuteronomy 4:29; Psalm 105:3–4).

title: Isaiah 31:1–3
content_html: Isaiah delivered another vision regarding Judah’s negotiations with Egypt, which probably took place in 705–701 BC. During that time, the conquering Assyrians were on the move, threatening Judah. At first, Hezekiah tried political and military means to avert being totally conquered (see 2 Kings 18:13–16).

title: Isaiah 31:4–9
content_html: This prophecy concerned God’s protection of Jerusalem and its rescue from the Assyrians.

title: Isaiah 31:9
content_html:
• princes will panic: This happened when Sennacherib ran to Nineveh after God destroyed 185,000 troops (see Isaiah 37:36–37).

  • God was the fire or flame that would consume Assyria.

title: Isaiah 32:1
content_html:
• a king will reign in righteousness: This means the Messiah, God's chosen one (Isaiah 11:1). The focus is now on righteous and wise leadership (Proverbs 8:20).

  • In Isaiah’s time, honest leaders were rare (see Isaiah 3:1–6; 28:7–19).

title: Isaiah 32:2
content_html: like a shelter … a refuge: These words also describe God's protection (Isaiah 25:4) and his gift of life-giving water (41:18; 43:19–20). The leaders would act as God's agents to bless his people.

title: Isaiah 32:5
content_html: Only foolish people value fools and scoundrels as heroes (see also Isaiah 19:11, 13; 32:6–8).

title: Isaiah 32:6
content_html: Fools ignore God's standards of justice and righteousness. They do not care about people who are hungry or thirsty (see Isaiah 1:17; 58:7, 10).

title: Isaiah 32:7
content_html: weapons … plots: See Proverbs 6:12, 18; 16:27; 24:8–9.

title: Isaiah 32:9
content_html: The women of Jerusalem live comfortably. They rely on wealth and status to keep their lifestyle, despite having low morals.

title: Isaiah 32:9–15
content_html: This prophecy warned the complacent women of Jerusalem. God's promises for the future did not excuse ongoing sin.

title: Isaiah 32:10
content_html:
In a little more than a year: This refers to events that were about to happen soon.

At that time, the Assyrian Empire was preparing to attack Jerusalem. The siege of Jerusalem in 701 BC was approaching.

title: Isaiah 32:11
content_html: People wore sackcloth to show grief, especially during mourning (see Psalm 30:11). The Assyrian conquest of Judah's farmlands and most cities, except Jerusalem, would cause wealthy city dwellers to mourn.

title: Isaiah 32:14
content_html: The Babylonians, not the Assyrians, eventually destroyed Jerusalem (see Isaiah 39:1–8 for a prediction of this).

title: Isaiah 32:15
content_html:
• The Spirit will change the nation into a godly community, starting a time of justice and righteousness (see also Joel 2:28–32). The Spirit is connected to the Messiah, God's chosen one (Isaiah 11:1) and the servant (42:1; 61:1).

  • desert … orchard: When the people are transformed, nature will be too.

title: Isaiah 32:17
content_html: Being right with God and people will bring peace (Hebrew shalom; see Isaiah 26:3; 48:18; 52:7; compare James 3:18). Peace is more than just the absence of conflict. It includes personal wholeness and does not rely on outside circumstances (see also Romans 5:1).

title: Isaiah 32:18
content_html: Living safely and feeling at peace were blessings of Israel's agreement with the Lord (see Leviticus 26:5–6). However, the people's sins had brought curses instead of blessings (compare Isaiah 32:9–11).

title: Isaiah 32:19
content_html: the forest, and the city: The faithful will stay safe, even when signs of God's judgment are everywhere.

title: Isaiah 33:1
content_html:
• This was the sixth warning of woe (see study note on Isaiah 28:1–33:24).

  • Although the Hebrew text does not specifically name Assyria as the object of the prophecy in this chapter, Isaiah likely referred to Assyria. They were the destroyer closest at the time the prophecy was written. The prophecy applies to anyone who tries to destroy God's people, including the Babylonians (see Isaiah 13–14).

  • When you have finished betraying: Wicked nations often break political agreements when these deals no longer benefit them.

title: Isaiah 33:2
content_html: The faithful community prayed for God to be merciful in response to the promise of Isaiah 30:18.

title: Isaiah 33:9
content_html: Sharon, Bashan, and Carmel were all fertile areas. Their desolation represents the destruction Assyria had caused in Israel.

title: Isaiah 33:11
content_html: In most passages, the Assyrians reduced other nations to worthless dry grass and stubble (see Isaiah 17:13; 29:5; 40:24; 41:2). Here, ironically, they experience the same fate.

title: Isaiah 33:14
content_html:
• The godless were people who lived without considering God and his laws.

  • The image of a consuming fire comes from God’s judgment of wrongdoers during the wilderness journey (Exodus 24:17; Deuteronomy 4:24). God still expects people to worship him with respect and awe (Hebrews 12:29).

title: Isaiah 33:15
content_html: Living a righteous life shows that a person truly knows God (see Colossians 3:5–17; James 2:14–18; 1 Peter 1:14–16; 1 John 1:5–6).

title: Isaiah 33:17
content_html:
• The king is the human representative (32:1) of the Great King, God himself (33:22).

  • The land belonging to God’s people would appear to be without borders because the foreign enemies had been judged (54:3).

title: Isaiah 33:17–24
content_html: This vision is about God’s reign in Zion.

title: Isaiah 33:18
content_html: Assyrian officers brought great terror to Judah when the Assyrians defeated various cities around Jerusalem (2 Kings 18:13).

title: Isaiah 33:19
content_html: The strange, unknown language was the language spoken in Assyria and later in Babylon (see Isaiah 28:11).

title: Isaiah 33:24
content_html:
• The people of Israel were the people of Zion (compare Isaiah 33:20).

  • sick … will be forgiven of iniquity: See Isaiah 43:25.

title: Isaiah 34:1–17
content_html: This prophecy of judgment was against the nations at the terrifying day of the Lord (see study note on Isaiah 2:5–22; see also Isaiah 63:1–6).

title: Isaiah 34:2
content_html: devote them to destruction: See Leviticus 27:28–29.

title: Isaiah 34:3
content_html: Leaving a dead body unburied was shameful.

title: Isaiah 34:4
content_html: The skies … stars: See also Isaiah 13:10; Joel 2:30–31; Matthew 24:29; Mark 13:24–25; 2 Peter 3:10–13; Revelation 6:13–14.

title: Isaiah 34:5
content_html: Edom represents what all nations will experience, similar to how Moab was described in Isaiah 25.

title: Isaiah 34:6
content_html: Bozrah was a city in Edom, southeast of the Dead Sea.

title: Isaiah 34:8
content_html:
• The Lord’s revenge means God’s just anger against his enemies and those who oppress his people on the day of the Lord.

  • a year of recompense: There will be payback for Edom’s wrongs against Judah (see also Psalm 137:7; Lamentations 4:21; Obadiah; Malachi 1:3–5). Edom, known for its betrayal, will meet its end.

title: Isaiah 34:9
content_html: Edom’s judgment was similar to the judgment of Sodom and Gomorrah (Genesis 19:24; see also Jeremiah 49:17–18).

title: Isaiah 34:10
content_html: See also Revelation 14:11; 19:3.

title: Isaiah 34:11
content_html: The identification of some of these birds is uncertain, but they inhabited wild and deserted places and many were considered unclean (Deuteronomy 14:11–18).

title: Isaiah 34:11–14
content_html: Wild animals, nocturnal birds, and thorny plants with thistles and briars filled the land. These things made the place feel empty and frightening.

title: Isaiah 34:16
content_html:
• Search and read the scroll of the LORD: Isaiah's prophecy showed that God's word is true.

  • The power of the Spirit makes the Lord's plans happen, ensuring all prophecy is fulfilled as God promised.

title: Isaiah 35:1
content_html: God's land would become a barren wasteland, both spiritually and physically. However, God would transform the desert into a garden (see also Isaiah 32:15), bringing his people from exile to glory.

title: Isaiah 35:1–10
content_html: God will come to defend and change his people. They will no longer be deaf, blind, or lame (see Isaiah 6:10). Instead, they will be pure, holy, and saved.

title: Isaiah 35:2
content_html:
• Lebanon … Carmel … Sharon: There will be a reversal of earlier judgments (see Isaiah 2:13; 10:34; 29:17; 33:9).

  • glory … splendor of our God: See also Isaiah 2:3; 40:5.

title: Isaiah 35:3
content_html: Limp hands and feeble knees express discouragement and anxiety (see Hebrews 12:12).

title: Isaiah 35:4
content_html:
• do not fear!: The Lord assures his people of his presence and purpose (see also Isaiah 7:4; 10:24; 40:9; 43:1; 44:2; 54:4).

  • The main message of the good news is that God is coming to save his people (see also Isaiah 40:9; 52:7; 62:11). God's arrival brings spiritual change and rescue from enemies.

title: Isaiah 35:5
content_html: eyes … ears: There will be a transformation from spiritual blindness and deafness to understanding and knowing God (Isaiah 6:10; 29:18; 32:3; 42:7; see also Luke 7:22; Acts 26:18).

title: Isaiah 35:6
content_html: the lame … and the mute: This promise shows that God will remove all disabilities. He will bring healing and wholeness (see Matthew 11:5; 12:22; Mark 7:37; Acts 3:7–8).

title: Isaiah 35:8
content_html:
• The image of the highway recalls Israel’s crossing through the Red Sea (Isaiah 51:10).

  • only those who walk: This means that only those who follow God will walk on his holy path. God's goal in salvation is for humans to share his character and act like him (see Ephesians 5:1–2; 1 Peter 2:21).

title: Isaiah 35:10
content_html: Zion: The prophecy goes beyond Israel's return from exile to the final establishment of God's kingdom on earth. This kingdom will be full of joy and happiness, with no sorrow or mourning (see also Isaiah 25:7; 51:11; Revelation 21:4).

title: Isaiah 36:1
content_html: King Sennacherib of Assyria ruled from 705 to 681 BC. Hezekiah, like many other kings under Assyrian control, declared his independence when Sennacherib became king during a difficult time. By 701 BC, Sennacherib was ready to punish Hezekiah. Sennacherib claimed he conquered 46 fortified cities and many villages, taking 200,146 captives. Hezekiah tried to appease Sennacherib (see 2 Kings 18:14–16), but it was too late.

title: Isaiah 36:1–39.8
content_html:
Jerusalem’s rescue from King Sennacherib of Assyria is a main event in Isaiah 1–39. The Lord promised to save his people. He would remain present with Jerusalem (see 7:14). He would also keep a remnant, a small group who would survive (see study note on Isaiah 10:20).

Much of the land became empty and the population greatly reduced. This happened because Judah made a foolish alliance with Assyria (chapters 7–8). Even so, the Lord did not allow total defeat. He preserved his people through Hezekiah, a faithful king.

Hezekiah faced the same kind of test that King Ahaz had faced. It was a test of trust in the Lord. In chapter 36, the Assyrian officer repeats the word trust as he challenges the people. But Hezekiah had learned from the earlier warnings in chapters 13–35. At least at first, he trusted God and passed the test.

This story also appears in 2 Kings 18:13–20:19.

title: Isaiah 36:2
content_html:
• Lachish was a city overlooking the hills west of Jerusalem. It needed to be captured before attacking Jerusalem.

  • The aqueduct that supplies water to the upper pool was on the north side of Jerusalem, not the Gihon Spring in the Kidron Valley.

  • Isaiah met Ahaz on this road leading to the field where cloth is washed about 33 years earlier (see Isaiah 7:3). At that time, he urged Ahaz to trust God. However, Ahaz trusted Assyria instead, leading to this desperate situation. Now, the challenge to trust God came from mocking foreign voices (see Isaiah 28:11–13).

title: Isaiah 36:3
content_html: Eliakim eventually advanced in rank, while Shebna was demoted (see Isaiah 22:15–24).

title: Isaiah 36:4
content_html: The title great king is similar to the title emperor.

title: Isaiah 36:4–22
content_html:
The Assyrian chief of staff tried to use intimidation to negotiate a peaceful settlement. In his first speech (Isaiah 36:4–10), he correctly argued that Egypt could not rescue them but incorrectly accused Hezekiah of misplaced trust in the Lord.

Interestingly, the Assyrian did not view the contest as between the gods of Assyria and the Lord, but rather between Sennacherib (the great king) and the Lord.

title: Isaiah 36:5
content_html: In whom are you now trusting: Judah had asked Egypt to help them (see Isaiah 30:1–5).

title: Isaiah 36:6
content_html:
• Such is Pharaoh king of Egypt to all who trust in him: Isaiah argued the same case, pointing to the Lord as the only reliable source of help (Isaiah 31:1–3, 7; 39:7).

  • A kind of reed that breaks easily grows near the Nile (see Ezekiel 29:6–7).

title: Isaiah 36:7
content_html:
• We trust in the LORD our God: Ironically, the Assyrian chief of staff knew about Isaiah's message and used it to his advantage (Isaiah 26:4; 36:15; 37:10).

  • Hezekiah showed his dedication to the Lord by tearing down the pagan shrines and altars (2 Kings 18:4). However, the Assyrians mistakenly thought Hezekiah had angered God by doing this.

title: Isaiah 36:8
content_html: At this time, cavalry mounted on horses was the newest military technology. The Assyrian chief of staff was mocking the Judeans because he knew that Judah had no trained men who knew how to ride.

title: Isaiah 36:10
content_html: The LORD Himself said to me: It was common for a king to claim that a god approved of their actions when they destroyed other kingdoms. This matched Isaiah’s prophecy in Isaiah 10:5–6.

title: Isaiah 36:11
content_html: Aramaic was the common language of the Assyrian Empire. The elite understood it, but the common people of Judah did not.

title: Isaiah 36:13–20
content_html: In the Assyrian chief of staff’s second speech, he claimed that his nation’s gods were more powerful than the God of Judah (compare Isaiah 10:9–10). The ability of God to rescue his people was in question. In the following events, the Lord surprised the Assyrian with his power (Isaiah 37:36–37).

title: Isaiah 36:14
content_html: Do not let Hezekiah deceive you: This public accusation against Hezekiah aimed to weaken the people's trust in him.

title: Isaiah 36:15
content_html: Hezekiah showed strong faith in the Lord, unlike Ahaz (Isaiah 7). Hezekiah’s faith led to rescue, while Ahaz’s lack of faith led to trouble (see Isaiah 7:9).

title: Isaiah 36:16–17
content_html: The Assyrian tried to lure the people of Jerusalem with supplies that the Lord promised to give to his obedient followers (see Isaiah 1:19; 37:30–35; Deuteronomy 8:7–9). It was a tempting offer, but it was fake. It hid the truth of the exile that would have come after (Isaiah 36:17).

title: Isaiah 36:19
content_html: The people of Hamath and Arpad were moved to Samaria, where they worshipped the gods of Sepharvaim (2 Kings 17:24). This argument made sense to an Assyrian. If the Assyrian gods defeated the gods of other nations and the God of the Israelites in Samaria, would they not also defeat the God of Judah?

title: Isaiah 37:1
content_html:
• Hezekiah responded to the threats by seeking the Lord’s favor.

  • The king tore his clothes and wore sackcloth as a sign of mourning and prayer.

title: Isaiah 37:4
content_html: Perhaps … God will hear the words of the Rabshakeh … defy the living God: Hezekiah's hope was not based on Judah's or his own goodness, nor on the presence of the temple in Jerusalem. Instead, it was based on God responding to Assyria's disrespect.

title: Isaiah 37:5–7
content_html: God answered that he would deal with the Assyrians and rescue the remnant of his people from their distress (see Isaiah 63:9).

title: Isaiah 37:8
content_html: Libnah was a city on the Philistine plain near Lachish. Sennacherib was preparing to fight both Egypt and Jerusalem when he sent word to Hezekiah.

title: Isaiah 37:9
content_html: King Tirhakah of Ethiopia was then the ruler of Egypt.

title: Isaiah 37:12
content_html:
• The argument was that Judah's god, like the gods of other nations already conquered by Assyria, would not be able to save Judah (see also Isaiah 36:18–20).

  • Gozan, Haran, Rezeph, Eden, and Telassar were cities in Mesopotamia.

title: Isaiah 37:14–20
content_html: When Hezekiah received Sennacherib’s boastful threat, he went back to the temple and prayed for help. His prayer was different from Ahaz’s reaction to danger decades earlier (see Isaiah 7).

title: Isaiah 37:16
content_html:
• God of Israel: Hezekiah did not believe Sennacherib's false claims that the Lord was powerless. In his prayer, Hezekiah recognized that the Lord, as the creator, is the God of all the earth's kingdoms. The Lord rules over all kingdoms.

  • The mighty cherubim were in the temple on top of the ark of the covenant, which was God's throne in his temple (see study note on Leviticus 16:2; see also Psalms 80:1; 99:1).

title: Isaiah 37:20
content_html: You alone, O LORD, are God: Hezekiah cared about the Lord's reputation, which Sennacherib had insulted. Hezekiah understood that if the Lord defeated the Assyrians and their gods, His holy name would be honored worldwide (see Exodus 9:16; 14:4, 17–18; Joshua 2:9–11; 1 Samuel 4:7–8).

title: Isaiah 37:21
content_html:
Because you have prayed: Prayer has great power. God heard Hezekiah’s prayer and responded.

The Lord answered him with a message of hope.

title: Isaiah 37:22
content_html: The Virgin Daughter of Zion: Jerusalem is described as a young woman who taunts the Assyrian king (see also Isaiah 3:26).

title: Isaiah 37:23
content_html: Against whom: Sennacherib’s speech was not so much an insult against Judah as it was against the Lord.

title: Isaiah 37:24
content_html: Isaiah seems to be familiar with the typical royal Assyrian boasts, including that they possessed the highest mountains. These words sound very much like those found in the annals of the Assyrian kings inscribed on the walls of their temples.

title: Isaiah 37:25
content_html: I have dug wells … I have dried up all the streams of Egypt: Sennacherib's boasts show his attitude of independence. However, the Lord alone is sovereign over nature (Isaiah 42:15; 43:19; 44:27).

title: Isaiah 37:26
content_html: I planned … that you should crush … into piles of rubble: Assyria was God's agent of destruction, but the Assyrians did not understand they were just a tool in God's hand (see Isaiah 10), carrying out what God had planned long ago (Isaiah 14:24–27).

title: Isaiah 37:28
content_html: your raging against Me: Assyria was completely hostile to the Lord, which led to even greater condemnation for them.

title: Isaiah 37:29
content_html: My hook … My bit: The Assyrians would be taken away like animals, just as they had done to many of their captives.

title: Isaiah 37:30
content_html:
This year … the second year … the third year: Due to the Assyrian siege and its impact on farming, the people of Judah could not plant and harvest as usual. The promise that life would return to normal after the siege reassured them that God was with them and would provide for their daily needs. They needed to build their trust in God over three years, believing that God's word would come true (see Isaiah 30:15, 18).

The idea was that after escaping the Assyrians, it would be too late to plant in the current year. They would have to wait until the fall of the second year to plant again and would harvest their first crop in the spring and summer of the third year.

title: Isaiah 37:30–35
content_html:
In this prophecy of salvation, Isaiah assured Hezekiah that Jerusalem would be spared and that the remnant was under God’s protection.

The names of Isaiah and his sons anticipated God's rescue (see Isaiah 7:1–11:16). The book’s record of God’s presence and rescue provided assurance that the Lord would always have a remnant that he will protect and rescue.

title: Isaiah 37:32
content_html: The passionate commitment of God includes his energetic zeal to keep his promise, the best guarantee his people could ever have (see also Isaiah 9:7).

title: Isaiah 37:33
content_html: He will not enter … arrow … shield … siege ramp: Despite their advanced military technology and great power, the Assyrians did not succeed against Jerusalem.

title: Isaiah 37:35
content_html: for the sake of My servant David: God had made a promise to David. He said that David’s royal family would continue forever (see Isaiah 9:6–7; 2 Samuel 7:8–17).

title: Isaiah 37:36
content_html:
• The angel of the Lord was a special heavenly messenger through whom God acted on earth. Often, he delivered important messages (see Genesis 16:7–14), but sometimes he brought judgment (see 2 Samuel 24:16).

  • struck down 185,000 men: The Lord began to fulfill his repeated promises: rescue of Jerusalem and judgment of Assyria (see Isaiah 10:16, 33–34; 30:31; 31:8).
