title: Isaiah 37:38
content_html:
• One day: Sennacherib was likely killed in 681 BC, about twenty years after he left Jerusalem.

  • Esarhaddon ruled Assyria from 680 to 669 BC. It is ironic that Sennacherib, who mocked the Lord, was killed by his sons in the temple of his own god.

title: Isaiah 38:1
content_html: In those days: This is a very general time statement. Hezekiah’s illness and healing likely happened before Jerusalem’s rescue (Isaiah 36–37), even though Isaiah mentions it afterward. Merodach-baladan’s rule in Babylon (Isaiah 39:1) ended before Sennacherib’s invasion in 701 BC. The text order suggests a link between these events (see Isaiah 38:6).

title: Isaiah 38:1–9
content_html:
The story of Hezekiah’s healing connects two events. It links the extension of Hezekiah’s life with Jerusalem’s rescue from the Assyrians.

These events happened before Jerusalem was saved. However, the book places them in this order because of their theme, not their time sequence. One main theme is the power of prayer (see Isaiah 37:14–20; 38:2–3). The Lord truly listens.

Hezekiah learned to trust God. His father, Ahaz, had refused to trust the Lord. Because Hezekiah trusted God, Judah and Jerusalem were spared complete destruction by the Assyrians.

Still, Hezekiah was not the promised Messiah, the Son of David (Isaiah 7:14; 9:1–7; 11:1–16; 16:5; 32:1; 33:17–22). He was a faithful king, but he was also human and would die. His failures and his sickness (chapters 38–39) prepare the reader for the coming of the true Messiah described in chapters 40–66.

title: Isaiah 38:3
content_html: I have walked before You faithfully: Hezekiah’s heart (his inner attitude and will) was right with the Lord for most of his reign (2 Kings 18:1–5). However, Hezekiah showed weakness when he relied on Egypt and did not act faithfully (Isaiah 31:1–9).

title: Isaiah 38:5
content_html: the God of your father David: Hezekiah followed David's example with faithfulness (see Isaiah 37:35; 38:1–22).

title: Isaiah 38:6
content_html: I will deliver you … I will defend this city: This promise refers to saving Jerusalem (Isaiah 36–37). It also helps us understand Hezekiah’s illness and recovery. Just as Hezekiah gained 15 more years, Jerusalem also received a temporary relief (see Isaiah 39).

title: Isaiah 38:9–20
content_html: Hezekiah’s poem includes both lament (Isaiah 38:9–14) and praise (38:15–20). The praise remains restrained until the final verse.

title: Isaiah 38:10
content_html: go through the gates of Sheol: This image comes from an ancient belief that people enter death through gates (see Isaiah 14:9). Old Testament believers did not understand the afterlife as the New Testament describes it. They saw it as a shadowy place where all the dead gathered, and where no one praised God (see Isaiah 38:18; Psalms 88:10; 115:17).

title: Isaiah 38:11
content_html: Hezekiah was very sad when he thought he might die. He believed that death would separate him from fellowship with God. He also feared losing time with his family and friends.

title: Isaiah 38:12
content_html: These images show how short human life is (compare 2 Corinthians 5:1). They remind us that life on earth does not last long.

title: Isaiah 38:15
content_html: I will walk slowly: Hezekiah might have once taken his life and health for granted. Now he understood that God could take these gifts away at any time.

title: Isaiah 38:16
content_html: God’s restoration of Hezekiah’s health also symbolized the restoration from exile that Judah would experience in the future.

title: Isaiah 38:21–22
content_html:
Hezekiah received a sign from the Lord. This sign reminds us of another sign that the prophet had offered to Ahaz (Isaiah 7:11).

Ahaz and Hezekiah stand at the two ends of a long story in Judah’s history. Ahaz represents the ungodly king. Hezekiah represents the godly king.

Ahaz brought disaster on his people through his choices. Hezekiah, in contrast, brought deliverance to his people. However, Isaiah 39 shows that even Hezekiah’s story includes warning and future trouble.

title: Isaiah 38:22
content_html: Hezekiah could not visit the temple with an infection (see Leviticus 13). So, going to the temple means he is healthy again.

title: Isaiah 39:1
content_html: Merodach-baladan, the king of Babylon from 722 to 710 and 704 to 703 BC, planned to rebel against Assyria. He sought help from Hezekiah. His envoys visited after Hezekiah recovered from a serious illness but before Sennacherib's final attack in 701 BC.

title: Isaiah 39:1–8
content_html: Hezekiah was an example of godliness, prayer, and care for the people of Jerusalem. However, he disappointed the Lord by showing off his wealth and military power to the envoys from Babylon.

title: Isaiah 39:2
content_html: silver … precious oils … his entire armory: By showing these treasures, Hezekiah demonstrated to Babylon's envoys that he had the resources to be a valuable ally.

title: Isaiah 39:6–7
content_html:
carried off to Babylon: This prophecy came before the prophecy about saving Jerusalem (Isaiah 37:35). Although this judgment and exile seem like a surprising end to Hezekiah's story, the exile of Israel and Judah was always a possibility (Isaiah 10:3–4, 20–23).

The Lord promised to protect Judah and Jerusalem from the Assyrians in 701 BC, but he warned Isaiah that worse would happen if they did not change. God showed his power and reliability by defeating the Assyrians, but the people did not change. The promises of Isaiah 2:1–5 and 4:2–6 would only come true through judgment (4:4).

Until the exile cleansed the nation's wrongdoings (Isaiah 6:5–7), their mission to other nations would not succeed.

title: Isaiah 40:1–31
content_html:
This chapter introduces chapters 40–66. In this section, comfort means the result of God's merciful actions toward people in the future. It includes encouragement, strength, and acceptance.

God wants to rescue his people (Isaiah 40:1–11) and is fully capable of doing so (40:12–26). The exiles needed to trust God's promises and wait for their rescue (Isaiah 40:27–31; see also 30:15–18).

title: Isaiah 40:1–55.13
content_html: This section shares the good news of God's coming salvation. The Lord will support his people and judge His enemies. Salvation will occur through the arrival, suffering, and exaltation of the Lord's servant, the Messiah, Jesus Christ.

title: Isaiah 40:1–66.24
content_html: The rest of Isaiah offers a message of comfort and reveals God's character and his plans for Israel. As Isaiah 39:6–7 predicted, Judah would face judgment and exile after Isaiah's time. Throughout Isaiah 40–66, Isaiah prophesied as if the exile in Babylon had already occurred. Thus, the Babylonian exile is the context for understanding these chapters.

title: Isaiah 40:2
content_html:
• Speak tenderly: The prophet's message was to encourage Jerusalem with the good news of God's forgiveness, reconciliation, and restoration.

  • From the prophetic perspective, Israel’s sad days are gone because the exile has ended.

  • she has received from the hand of the LORD double for all her sins: Israel faced the full force of God's wrath (Isaiah 51:19–23; compare 61:7).

title: Isaiah 40:3
content_html:
• A voice of one calling: In Malachi 3:1, this person (possibly the Elijah of Malachi 4:5) was God's messenger who prepared the way for God's coming. In the Gospels, people identified this announcer as John the Baptist (Matthew 3:3; Mark 1:3; Luke 3:4; John 1:23).

  • Prepare the way … make a straight highway: The announcer's message would speed up God's coming (see Isaiah 35:8). As God made a way for Israel through the Red Sea (Isaiah 43:16), a way would also be opened for the Lord (see 52:11–12).

title: Isaiah 40:3–5
content_html:
There is now a shift from announcing the good news to expecting God's arrival. The good news (Isaiah 40:1–2) is based on God himself.

Isaiah described the Lord as coming on a highway through the desert (see Isaiah 35). However, the imagery is reversed here: In Isaiah 35, the people traveled to Zion, toward the Lord. Here, the Lord is coming toward his people (see also Isaiah 52:8, where both images are combined).

title: Isaiah 40:5
content_html:
• The future display of God's glory would include Judah's return from exile but extend beyond that. The New Testament confirms that God's glory was revealed in Jesus Christ (John 1:14; Hebrews 1:3).

  • the mouth of the LORD has spoken: The promised salvation and judgment will surely happen because the Lord, who keeps his promises, has declared it (see Isaiah 1:20; 58:14).

title: Isaiah 40:6
content_html:
• Cry out: So that everyone can hear.

  • People are not worthy of the revelation of God’s glory because they are mortal.

title: Isaiah 40:6–8
content_html: All humans are mortal and temporary. In contrast, God continues with his plans and succeeds. Time does not stop him from doing what he wants (see Isaiah 14:24).

title: Isaiah 40:7
content_html: The breath of the LORD refers to God's spoken words of judgment (see Isaiah 4:4).

title: Isaiah 40:8
content_html: the word of our God stands forever: All of God’s words, including his plans and the written word, will succeed. God might change human expectations, but everything will follow his wise plan (see Isaiah 14:24; 55:8–11; see also Matthew 25:34).

title: Isaiah 40:9
content_html: God is coming to save and restore human beings (see also Isaiah 35:4; Revelation 22:12).

title: Isaiah 40:9–11
content_html: God is the good news. He comes as a warrior to rescue his people with power, yet he also holds them gently like a shepherd.

title: Isaiah 40:10
content_html:
• The Lord's rule is different from that of unjust and weak rulers, whom he will judge. His rule is compassionate, just, righteous, and powerful.

  • His arm establishes His rule: God showed his power in events such as:

    • the defeat of the Egyptians (Isaiah 63:12),

    • the judgment of his enemies (Isaiah 30:30; 48:14; 51:9), and

    • the rescue of his people (Isaiah 59:16).

title: Isaiah 40:11
content_html: Isaiah used the well-known biblical image of a shepherd to describe God's care for his people (see also Isaiah 49:10; Psalm 23:1; Jeremiah 3:15; 23:4; Ezekiel 34:11–17; Matthew 2:6; John 10:1–18).

title: Isaiah 40:12–13
content_html: These rhetorical questions suggest the answer, “No one!” God alone created everything. He will judge creation (Isaiah 24:17), and only he can and will restore it through his salvation.

title: Isaiah 40:12–17
content_html: In response to Israel's questions (spoken by the prophet), the Lord declared that he is the unmatched God, a wise and sovereign creator who governs the nations.

title: Isaiah 40:15
content_html: God rules over all nations and their power structures. Compared to God, they are almost nothing. This includes the Babylonian Empire, which was just a tool in God's hand.

title: Isaiah 40:16
content_html: People valued Lebanon for its plentiful wood and wildlife (see Isaiah 2:13–16; Psalm 104:16–17).

title: Isaiah 40:17
content_html: The nations, including their leaders (Isaiah 40:23), pagan structures (41:24, 29; 44:9–11), and all enemies of God's people (41:11–12), are worth nothing apart from God.

title: Isaiah 40:18
content_html:
• To whom … what image: Earlier in the book, Isaiah showed that idolatry is foolish and empty (Isaiah 16:12; 37:16–19). Isaiah 40–48, he gives a much longer argument against it.

    An idol is an object that people make to represent a god or a religious idea. But these objects are made by human hands. Those who worship them do not see that they are only things people have made.

    Idols have no power (Isaiah 41:7, 22–24; 48:14). They give people a false feeling of safety (Isaiah 42:17). They mislead people and cause deep disappointment (Isaiah 44:20; 42:17; 45:16, 20). They cannot save or protect anyone.

    In fact, idols are so weak that they can fall over (Isaiah 40:20; 41:7). They compete with God’s glory (Isaiah 42:8). They also lower human dignity, because people bow down to things they have made (44:9; see also 41:21–29).

  • The Lord, the true God, is not like idols. No one can compare to him (see Isaiah 40:25; 46:5; Psalms 86:8; 89:6). He is completely unique and without equal.

title: Isaiah 40:20
content_html: an idol that will not topple: This is a sarcastic remark. It would be embarrassing if the idol could not even stand (see Isaiah 40:18; 41:7; 1 Samuel 5:4).

title: Isaiah 40:21
content_html: Idol worshipers do not recognize God or respond wisely to him. The faithful in Israel understand and see God's role in the story of redemption, which includes both exile and restoration (Isaiah 41:20).

title: Isaiah 40:22
content_html:
• God rules as king over the entire earth (see Isaiah 6:1; Psalms 2:4; 80:1; 99:1; 102:12; 113:5). God is so vast and powerful that humans seem like grasshoppers in comparison.

  • Believing in God as the creator of the heavens and earth opposes the common Babylonian and Egyptian belief that the sun, moon, and stars are gods.

  • like a curtain...a tent: See also Psalm 104:2.

title: Isaiah 40:23
content_html: princes: God is not impressed by human power or fame. Human strength and reputation do not change who God is.

title: Isaiah 40:25
content_html: The Holy One is a shorter form of the Holy One of Israel. This is a common name for God in the book of Isaiah (see also Isaiah 10:17; 43:15; Psalm 22:3).

title: Isaiah 40:26
content_html: Lift up your eyes on high: Just as God told Abraham to look into the sky (Genesis 15:5), he also told Israel to look to the stars for encouragement (see also Isaiah 51:2–3). The cosmos shows God's great power and unmatched strength. The Strong One can strengthen the weak (Isaiah 40:27–31; see Psalm 147:5).

title: Isaiah 40:27
content_html: My way is hidden … ignored by my God: The coming exile, a time when God's powerful presence and rescue would not be visible, would prompt questions (see also Isaiah 49:14; 54:7; Lamentations 5:20–22).

title: Isaiah 40:28
content_html:
• know … understood: Compare Isaiah 40:21.

  • the everlasting God, the Creator of the ends of the earth: Creation shows God's power and wisdom. He is also the Creator of the future world, a place of righteousness, justice, and peace (Isaiah 45:8; 48:6–7; 65:17–18).

  • He will not grow tired or weary: God does not have human limitations.

title: Isaiah 40:29
content_html: In this context, the weak are those who face persecution or oppression, like during the exile in Babylon. The Lord is their only help (see Isaiah 49:4–5; 50:4; Psalms 68:9; 119:28).

title: Isaiah 40:31
content_html:
• wait upon the LORD: See Isaiah 33:2; 49:23; 51:5.

  • like eagles: This strong image of rescue reminded readers of the exodus event from long ago (Exodus 19:4; Deuteronomy 32:10–12; see also Psalm 103:5).

title: Isaiah 41:1
content_html:
• The nations gathering for a trial must listen quietly to God's arguments.

  • Let them come forward and testify: Literally let them find new strength, which is a reference to Isaiah 40:31.

  • God invited the nations to come and speak, challenging them to counter his case.

title: Isaiah 41:1–7
content_html: The Lord invited the nations to a trial to show that he alone is God. They could not defend themselves as he announced that a king from the east (Isaiah 41:2) would defeat Babylon, allowing Israel to return.

title: Isaiah 41:2
content_html:
• The king from the east was Cyrus, the Persian king who conquered Babylon in 539 BC. He allowed the Jews to return to their land and rebuild Jerusalem in 538 BC (Ezra 1:1–4).

    Isaiah’s prophecies about Cyrus (Isaiah 44:28–45:13; 48:14–15) encouraged Israel to see the Lord as the ruler over all human history, including the actions of great kings.

  • He hands nations over to him: As victorious as the conquering Cyrus was, he was still subject to the Lord.

title: Isaiah 41:4
content_html:
• the generations from the beginning?: God rules over everything. From the very beginning, he has guided each stage of history according to his plan.

  • In the book of Revelation, Jesus calls himself the Alpha and the Omega, the First and the Last, the Beginning and the End (Revelation 1:8, 17; 2:8; 21:6; 22:13).

  • I am He: The Lord alone is God (see also Isaiah 43:10, 13; 46:4; 48:12; Deuteronomy 32:39). Jesus used similar expressions for himself (see John 6:35; 8:12, 24; 9:5; 10:7, 9, 11, 14; 11:25; 14:6; 15:1, 5).

title: Isaiah 41:5–7
content_html: This taunt against idol worship responded to God's raising up Cyrus. The nations hoped their idols would protect them.

title: Isaiah 41:6
content_html: Be strong!: The nations put their hope in things that have no strength, objects made by human craftsmen.

title: Isaiah 41:8
content_html:
• The servant, representing the nation of Israel, was God's chosen one, with a purpose based on God's plans.

  • Abraham My friend: In Hebrew, this phrase could mean Abraham who loves me or Abraham whom I love. (See also James 2:23).

title: Isaiah 41:8–16
content_html: These two prophecies about salvation for God's servant Israel (Isaiah 41:8–13, 14–16) prepare for the first of the suffering servant songs (see study note on Isaiah 42:1–4).

title: Isaiah 41:9
content_html:
• I have chosen you restates Isaiah 41:8 to emphasize God's commitment and faithfulness to the people of Israel, even though they had not been faithful to him.

  • not rejected you: During the exile in Babylon, the people felt that God had rejected them (see Isaiah 49:21; 54:6). However, God had good plans for them.

title: Isaiah 41:10
content_html:
• I am with you: God's promise of presence (see Isaiah 7:14) is central to the Bible. Because God is present, his people do not need to fear (see also Isaiah 43:1–2, 5).

  • I am your God: The Lord uses covenant language to affirm that he is their God and they are his people (see also Jeremiah 7:23; 31:1, 33; Ezekiel 14:11; 36:28; 37:27; Zechariah 8:8).

  • uphold you with My righteous right hand: The Lord uses language similar to the exodus (compare Exodus 15:6) to encourage Israel (see also Isaiah 41:13; 63:12).

title: Isaiah 41:14
content_html:
• In exile, Israel was in a lowly state.

  • God is the Redeemer. He works powerfully to rescue and restore people from sin and its consequences (for Israel, it was the exile; for an application to Jesus, see Luke 2:38; 21:28; Romans 3:24; 1 Corinthians 1:30; Galatians 4:5; Ephesians 1:1–14; Titus 2:14; Hebrews 9:12).

title: Isaiah 41:16
content_html: When the grain is thrown into the wind, the chaff is blown away. Likewise, Israel’s enemies appeared to be strong but would easily be driven off.

title: Isaiah 41:18
content_html: rivers … fountains … a pool of water … springs: These pictures would have been very meaningful to people who lived by farming and raising animals. In an agrarian culture (a farming society), water meant life. Rivers, springs, and pools were signs of safety, blessing, and hope. These images also suggest that rescue from the exile in Babylon would be like a second exodus (compare Exodus 15:27; 17:6).

title: Isaiah 41:21
content_html: the King of Jacob: See Isaiah 6:5; 43:15; 44:6.

title: Isaiah 41:21–29
content_html: This trial scene develops the Lord’s case against idolatry. Idols are nothing but a human creation, whereas God is the Creator of all things. Idols cannot speak, act, accomplish anything, or save their worshipers.

title: Isaiah 41:25
content_html:
• I have raised up on: The God who planned the exile through Babylon also planned Israel’s return from exile through Cyrus (see Isaiah 41:2; see also 13:17).

  • Although Persia is to the east, travelers to Israel and Judah had to enter from the north due to rough terrain.

  • He will march over rulers … treads the clay: Just as Babylon trampled Assyria, Persia would trample Babylon.

title: Isaiah 41:26
content_html: Idols and false gods could not predict the future.

title: Isaiah 41:27
content_html: The messenger with good news announced that God would come to save his people (see Isaiah 40:9).

title: Isaiah 42:1
content_html:
• in whom My soul delights: God used similar words at the baptism of Jesus (Matthew 3:17).

  • I will put My Spirit on Him: Any leader might be called a servant, but the Spirit's presence suggests a king from David’s line or a prophet like Moses (see Numbers 11:17, 24–29).

  • Establishing justice is a king's duty (see Isaiah 9:6–7; Psalm 72:1).

  • This king’s mission will extend beyond governing Judah to a greater mission for the nations.

title: Isaiah 42:1–4
content_html: The servant here is not the people of Israel (as in Isaiah 41:8–28 and elsewhere in chapters 41–48; see 49:5–6). Instead, the servant is a royal figure who accomplishes his mission with care for people, especially for those who are hurting. The passage is the first of four songs about this servant (Isaiah 42:1–4; 49:1–13; 50:4–11; 52:13–53:12). He brings in an era of universal justice. For the connection of the servant with Jesus Christ, see Matthew 12:18–21.

title: Isaiah 42:2
content_html: cry out or raise His voice: The royal servant will speak with calm confidence in his message and calling from God (see also Isaiah 11:1–5).

title: Isaiah 42:3
content_html: The royal servant will be gentle with the oppressed and discouraged (see Isaiah 3:15; 41:17).

title: Isaiah 42:4
content_html: the islands: The nations want justice and guidance on how to achieve it. The servant is like Moses, who received the law. However, the servant is greater because he brings justice to all nations, not just Israel.

title: Isaiah 42:6
content_html:
• I will keep you … to be a covenant for the people: The people might refer to all the nations of the earth (see Isaiah 49:6).

  • a light to the nations: See also Isaiah 51:4; Acts 13:47.

title: Isaiah 42:7
content_html: The servant will help the spiritually blind see (see Isaiah 6:10; 29:18) and free spiritual captives from sin. This is in addition to freeing those held captive during the exile in Babylon (see Isaiah 61:1 with Luke 4:18).

title: Isaiah 42:8–9
content_html: I am the LORD: God created the world and only God can make predictions come true. God alone deserves glory and praise.

title: Isaiah 42:10–11
content_html:
• Different geographical regions show the extremes of where people live. The entire earth is invited to praise God for his promise to save humanity.

  • Kedar and Sela were examples of desert locations (Isaiah 42:11; see also 16:1; 21:16–17).

title: Isaiah 42:13
content_html:
• a mighty one … a warrior: This theme comes from God's victory at the Red Sea during the exodus (Exodus 15:3).

  • God will defeat all his enemies to save his people (Isaiah 51:9; 63:1–6; see also Psalms 54:7; 108:9; 112:8).

title: Isaiah 42:14
content_html: I have kept silent … and restrained: Israel felt God's absence during the exile in Babylon, which happened due to their idol worship (Isaiah 57:11–13; 2 Kings 17:6–24). The faithful will pray for God's presence and renewed involvement in their lives (Isaiah 64:12), and the Lord will answer their prayers because he is committed to their redemption (62:1, 6).

title: Isaiah 42:18–20
content_html: The Israelites who ignored God's words and actions became spiritually blind and deaf (see Isaiah 6:9–10). They knew the truth from God's word and the prophets, but their closed minds refused to respond.

title: Isaiah 42:21
content_html: magnify His law and make it glorious: God’s word is a witness to his righteous rule (see Isaiah 1:10).

title: Isaiah 42:22–25
content_html: Throughout its history, Israel's sins (Isaiah 42:24) made it vulnerable to foreign oppressors. Many nations, especially Assyria and Babylon, became tools God used to express his anger (Isaiah 42:25) toward his rebellious people.

title: Isaiah 42:25
content_html: Generation after generation of Israelites did not learn their lesson, even when God disciplined them through military defeat.

title: Isaiah 43:1
content_html:
• the LORD … who created you: The idea of creation (see study note on Isaiah 40:28) now refers to God forming the nation of Israel.

  • He who formed you: During the exodus, God freed his people from slavery. Similarly, God planned to bring his people back from exile to their land. Ultimately, Jesus gave his life as a ransom for all humanity (Matthew 20:28; 1 Timothy 2:6; Hebrews 9:15).

  • I have called you by your name; you are Mine!: Despite having faced his wrath, the people of Israel are still God’s chosen people.

title: Isaiah 43:1–7
content_html: In this promise of salvation, the Lord spoke directly to his people.

title: Isaiah 43:2
content_html:
• Conquering forces are compared to flooding rivers (see Isaiah 8:8).

  • I will be with you: God promises to stay with his people to protect and care for them (see Isaiah 7:14; 41:10; 43:5; 45:14).

  • Israel faced God's judgment as the fire of oppression during the exile in Babylon.

title: Isaiah 43:7
content_html: One of God’s purposes in restoring his people was to display his glory to the watching world.

title: Isaiah 43:8–13
content_html: The Lord here called on Israel, his blind servant, to be his star witness in a mock trial against idols and false gods. Israel knew that the Lord alone is God, and the people had experienced his salvation.

title: Isaiah 43:10
content_html: You are My witnesses: By their very presence in exile, Israel was evidence that God is truly God. He predicted the exile long beforehand, and now it had come to pass. God alone is the true God because he speaks and acts and controls all of history. Idols and false gods could do none of these things. Israel would later become even greater evidence because God had also predicted their redemption (Isaiah 43:11–12).

title: Isaiah 43:14–21
content_html: The Lord promised Israel that it would be freed from Babylon. This redemption would be similar to Israel's past freedom from Egypt but even greater in some ways (Isaiah 43:18).

title: Isaiah 43:18
content_html: God wanted the Israelites to remember the exodus from Egypt. However, they needed to focus on the amazing event that was about to happen, instead of staying stuck in the past.

title: Isaiah 43:21
content_html: One purpose of redemption is to honor God through the praises of the redeemed (see 1 Peter 2:9).

title: Isaiah 43:22
content_html: Israel's history of rebellion led them into many crises. Their rebellion was so strong that they refused to ask God for help. When they did pray, their wickedness often caused God not to hear their prayers (Isaiah 1:15).

title: Isaiah 43:25
content_html:
God alone can blot out sins. This means he completely removes and forgives them.

It does not matter how many sins a person has committed or how serious they are. God has the power to forgive them all (see also Isaiah 44:22).

title: Isaiah 43:27
content_html: spokesmen: God held the kings of Israel and Judah especially responsible for leading the nation into sin (see 2 Kings 21).

title: Isaiah 44:1–5
content_html:
The promise of salvation gave Israel a future. Even though the people had sinned, God would still use them to bless the next generation (Isaiah 44:3).

The past had been dark because of their sin (see Isaiah 43:22–28). But the future would be different. God would begin a new time of blessing. His Spirit would change the people and make this new beginning possible.

title: Isaiah 44:3
content_html:
• The restoration theme is connected with God’s promise to pour out the Spirit (see also Isaiah 32:15–17; Joel 2:28–32; Acts 2:16–18).

  • on your descendants … your offspring: The next generation of Israelites offered hope.

title: Isaiah 44:6–20
content_html: This passage uses the form of a trial speech to press God’s argument that disgrace comes to all who trust in idols and false gods.

title: Isaiah 44:15–17
content_html: He takes some of it to warm himself … He also fashions it into a god and worships it: This description of making an idol is full of sarcasm and mocks the foolishness of idol worshipers (see also Isaiah 44:19).

title: Isaiah 44:18–19
content_html: closed their minds: Idol worshipers do not see the irony in using one part of a log for warmth and another part as their god.

title: Isaiah 44:21–22
content_html: The Lord promised to forgive the sins of Israel.

title: Isaiah 44:23
content_html: All creation is called to celebrate God’s redemption of Israel (compare Isaiah 1:2).

title: Isaiah 44:24–45.8
content_html: The Lord alone is sovereign over history. He raised up Cyrus of Persia to execute judgment on Babylon and restore Israel to the Holy Land.

title: Isaiah 44:25
content_html: False prophets and diviners used forbidden methods to predict the future. Eventually, God would reveal them as liars who believed something that is not true (see Isaiah 3:2; 9:15; 47:13; Deuteronomy 18:10–11).

title: Isaiah 44:27
content_html: The sudden loss of natural resources sometimes shows divine judgment (see Isaiah 42:15; compare 43:20). Here, however, God's ability to dry up streams with only a word shows his power to fulfill his promises through Cyrus of Persia.

title: Isaiah 44:28
content_html: My shepherd: Cyrus did more than just allow the Jews to return home. He led a major project to rebuild Jerusalem and restore the temple (see Isaiah 45:13; Ezra 1:2–4; 6:3–5).

title: Isaiah 45:1
content_html:
• His anointed: This title, usually for David or the Messiah, is used here for Cyrus. Cyrus was anointed because he was chosen for a special mission. No other foreign ruler received this title (compare 1 Samuel 10:1; Romans 13:1).

  • whose right hand I have grasped: The Lord would help Cyrus defeat Babylon (see Isaiah 43:14).

title: Isaiah 45:3
content_html: so that you may know: Cyrus's victories show that the Lord can fulfill his plans. Idols and false gods cannot do this (see Isaiah 41:21–29).

title: Isaiah 45:4–5
content_html: I call you by name: Cyrus did not know the Lord, but the Lord knew him. Isaiah predicted the rescuer Cyrus by name 150 years before he was born. This prophecy would show the exiles that the Lord is truly God and that he knows the future.

title: Isaiah 45:7
content_html: light … darkness … prosperity … calamity: Everything is under God’s control.

title: Isaiah 45:9–11
content_html: pot … the potter … him … his father: It is foolish for a creature to question the wisdom of its creator.

title: Isaiah 45:9–13
content_html: The Lord addresses those who doubt his decision to use a non-believing king for his plans. As the Creator, he is free and has complete authority. He promised to return his people to their land and would use anyone he chooses to fulfill that promise.

title: Isaiah 45:14
content_html: Egypt … Cush … Sabeans: Israel's restoration would give them new sovereignty. Foreign nations would honor God. Recognizing God's blessing on His people, they would also honor them.

title: Isaiah 45:17
content_html: Israel will never again face humiliation, disgrace, oppression, harassment, or abuse. These declarations suggest this promise will be fulfilled at the end of time.

title: Isaiah 45:18
content_html: Ancient pagan people saw the world as a place of empty chaos. However, God's plans are good. He created the world from the start to live in harmony with him.

title: Isaiah 45:18–25
content_html: Salvation comes only from the Lord. He is the creator, the revealer, and the one who carries out his will in human history. He promised to start a new era of salvation and righteousness (see Isaiah 45:8). Every person will submit to God, either by choice or not (Isaiah 45:23–24).

title: Isaiah 45:22
content_html:
• Turn to Me … all the ends of the earth: All people need to seek the Lord, the true God who created everything, while he is available (Isaiah 55:6).

  • In God there is salvation, the only hope for all people (Isaiah 45:8).

title: Isaiah 45:23
content_html:
• God's words are always true. When God swears by his own name, it shows he will never break his promise (see also Isaiah 14:24; 54:9; 62:8; Hebrews 6:13).

  • Every knee … every tongue: At the end of time, all nations will accept his authority, either willingly or unwillingly (see Romans 14:11; 1 Corinthians 15:25–27; Philippians 2:10–11).

title: Isaiah 45:24
content_html: In this context, the people include everyone who seeks salvation from the Lord, even those from other nations (Isaiah 45:22).

title: Isaiah 45:25
content_html: justified: Literally righteous (see also Isaiah 45:8, 24).

title: Isaiah 46:1
content_html:
• Marduk-bel was the most important god in the Babylonian group of gods. Bel, similar to Baal, meant lordship.

  • Nebo (Nabu, Nebu), the patron god of Babylonian scribes, was believed to be the son of Marduk-bel.

  • Instead of being celebrated at a Babylonian festival, people would transport these gods on ox carts and discard them, as the Babylonian people rejected them.

title: Isaiah 46:1–2
content_html:
This taunt was against the self-contradiction of Babylonian religion.

A taunt is a mocking song or speech. In this passage, the prophet makes fun of Babylon’s beliefs. The message shows that their religion does not make sense.

title: Isaiah 46:3
content_html:
• the remnant of the house of Israel: This refers to the survivors after Jerusalem's destruction in 586 BC.

  • The Lord, the Creator of humanity, cares for and carries his people, unlike the Babylonian idols that need carrying.

  • sustained from the womb, carried along since birth: The Lord chose Israel even before it became a nation (see also Isaiah 49:5).

title: Isaiah 46:8–13
content_html: Many exiles felt hopeless and doubted God's promise to rescue them. God addressed this doubt.

title: Isaiah 46:10
content_html: God knows the future before it happens; he controls all events, and his actions match his words (Isaiah 41:4, 26). He is the first and the last (Isaiah 44:6; 48:12).

title: Isaiah 46:11
content_html: The swift bird of prey from the east refers to Cyrus, the Persian king (see study note on Isaiah 41:2).

title: Isaiah 46:12
content_html: Formerly, Israel cared little for doing right (literally righteousness; see also Isaiah 58:2; 59:9).

title: Isaiah 46:13
content_html:
• The good news for Israel was that God was coming to make things right (literally I will bring my righteousness near; see Isaiah 40:9; 51:5).

  • adorn Israel with My splendor: Unlike a craftsman who gives material splendor to an idol (Isaiah 44:13), the Lord gives true dignity to his people.

title: Isaiah 47:1
content_html: To sit in the dust was a way of expressing humiliation.

title: Isaiah 47:1–4
content_html:
In this taunt against Babylon, the nation is described as a proud queen.

But she is brought low and humiliated. Instead of ruling, she is forced to work like a servant girl.

title: Isaiah 47:1–48.22
content_html:
We can draw two conclusions from the earlier evidence that the Lord alone is God:

  1. Babylon's great pride is foolish (Isaiah 47:1–15);

  2. Israel should listen to the word of the Lord (Isaiah 48:1–22).

title: Isaiah 47:3
content_html: God will bring justice to the world by punishing those who oppose him and oppress his people (see Isaiah 34:8). This will remove wickedness.

title: Isaiah 47:5–11
content_html: Babylon was known for its confidence and arrogance (see Isaiah 14:13–17; Daniel 4:30). The nation's charms, magic, and wisdom could not stop the disaster planned for it.

title: Isaiah 47:6
content_html: The people of Israel had provoked God’s wrath, prompting him to use Babylon to punish them. Now wicked Babylon itself would be punished.

title: Isaiah 47:8
content_html:
• I am, and there is none besides me: This was a claim to divinity (compare Isaiah 45:5; 47:10). Only the Lord can make such a claim. It is extremely arrogant for any created thing, including a nation, to say this.

  • Being a widow or losing one's children was considered a disgrace.

title: Isaiah 47:9
content_html: People often used witchcraft and magic to protect themselves from evil and ensure a happy future.

title: Isaiah 47:10
content_html: Here, wisdom and knowledge likely refer sarcastically to the false knowledge from occult practices. This false knowledge led Babylon away from the truth and into disaster (Isaiah 47:11).

title: Isaiah 47:12–15
content_html: When disaster struck, Babylon turned to occult practices for help, but found none.

title: Isaiah 47:13
content_html: Babylon was advanced in astronomy, which the astrologers and stargazers used as a form of divination (see Daniel 2:2, 10; 5:7).

title: Isaiah 48:1
content_html: Jacob … Judah: Isaiah usually focused on Judah, but here he spoke to all the tribes of Israel.

title: Isaiah 48:1–2
content_html: Israel was religious but not truly committed to the Lord.

title: Isaiah 48:1–11
content_html: This prophecy is a disputation, a speech in which the prophet argues a set of points against his audience.

title: Isaiah 48:1–22
content_html: The Lord urges Israel to abandon its long history of rebellion and pay attention to his words. In Hebrew, the word for listening often means the same as obeying or acting on what is heard.

title: Isaiah 48:3
content_html: The Lord had warned Israel about what was going to happen in the exile.

title: Isaiah 48:6–7
content_html: Although Israel often did not believe God's past predictions, God revealed new things they had not heard. Specifically, God revealed that Babylon would fall and Israel would be restored.

title: Isaiah 48:10
content_html: The exile in Babylon was a period of intense suffering (compare Deuteronomy 4:20) that God used to purify Israel.

title: Isaiah 48:11
content_html: For My own sake … I will act: Israel's exile made other nations question the Lord's character and reputation. These nations did not see that Israel's wickedness caused the exile. Instead, they saw it as a failure of Israel's God to protect his people and their land (see Isaiah 36:19–20; 37:12; Ezekiel 36:19–26).

title: Isaiah 48:12–22
content_html: This final trial speech reviews the arguments of Isaiah 40–48 and presents the final conclusion.

title: Isaiah 48:14
content_html: chosen ally: See Isaiah 44:28; 45:1–2.

title: Isaiah 48:15
content_html: I have brought him, and he will succeed in his mission: Cyrus would humiliate Babylon and liberate Israel (Isaiah 45:1–4).

title: Isaiah 48:16
content_html:
• I have not spoken in secret: God's promises are clear, though they do not always specify how or when they will happen.

  • And now the Lord GOD has sent me, accompanied by His Spirit: The identity of the one sent is unclear. Isaiah might be referring to himself as a prophet inspired by the Spirit, speaking God's words to Israel. The connection with the spirit suggests it refers to the promised servant, the Messiah.

  • The presence of the Spirit ensures the servant's success.

title: Isaiah 48:18
content_html:
• Peace and righteousness will be established when the kingdom of God fully comes (see Isaiah 66:12).

  • like a river … like waves of the sea: There will be abundant peace and overflowing righteousness (compare Amos 5:24). Israel could have had this if its people had listened to God.

title: Isaiah 48:19
content_html: Your descendants … as the sand: This fulfills God's promise to Abraham (see Genesis 22:17). In contrast, the Israelites were almost destroyed during the exile in Babylon (see Isaiah 44:26).

title: Isaiah 48:20
content_html: Leave Babylon: See also Isaiah 52:11–12; 55:12.

title: Isaiah 48:21
content_html: not thirst … rock … water gushed out: These reminders of God’s provision in the exodus provide a fitting summary of what he would do to rescue Israel from captivity in Babylon (see Numbers 20:11).

title: Isaiah 48:22
content_html: This phrase, There is not peace … for the wicked (also Isaiah 57:21), highlights the clear difference between the godly and the wicked (see 66:24). Peace represents God's kingdom, which does not include the wicked.

title: Isaiah 49:1
content_html:
• O distant peoples: The servant's work would not be limited to Israel. His ministry would reach people in faraway nations (see Isaiah 42:4, 10, 12; 51:5; 60:9; 66:19).

  • called Me from the womb … He named Me: The promised servant's prophetic call was like Jeremiah's call (see Jeremiah 1:5; see also Galatians 1:15). It was not a general call but a very specific, personal one (see also Isaiah 43:1; 45:3–4).

title: Isaiah 49:1–13
content_html: This is traditionally seen as the second of four songs about a suffering servant (see study note on Isaiah 42:1–4). The servant's role in suffering becomes clearer (see also Isaiah 50:4–9; 52:13–53:12).

title: Isaiah 49:1–55.13
content_html:
God planned to rescue the Israelites from exile. But an important question remained. How could sinful Israel serve the Holy One again?

God would make a way to remove Israel’s sin. He would restore their fellowship with him. This means he would bring them back into a right relationship with himself.

The way would be through the promised Servant. This Servant would come for Israel and for all nations. He was first introduced in Isaiah 42:1–9. n this passage, he becomes the main focus.

title: Isaiah 49:2
content_html: My mouth: The servant's mission would be prophetic (see Isaiah 11:4; Ephesians 6:17; Hebrews 4:12; Revelation 1:16; 2:12, 16; 19:15).

title: Isaiah 49:3
content_html:
The suffering servant would emerge from Israel (see Isaiah 41:8) and would be the true Israel. He would serve as both king (Isaiah 42:1) and prophet (Isaiah 49:1). He would be anointed with the Spirit of God (Isaiah 61:1). God would appoint him (Isaiah 48:16) as a faithful witness to God's plans (43:12; 48:20).

In his mission to both Israel and other nations (Isaiah 49:6), the suffering servant would face rejection and disgrace while remaining faithful, unlike the nation of Israel (see Isaiah 50:4–9). His suffering would be for the sake of God's chosen people.

Jesus became the ultimate fulfillment of the suffering servant (see study note on Isaiah 52:13–53:12).

title: Isaiah 49:4
content_html:
• I have labored in vain: Even though people stubbornly refuse his message, the servant will still reach out to them.

  • My vindication is with the LORD: Despite discouragement and suffering, the servant will continue to perform his duty faithfully, trusting God for the outcome. God promised to reward the servant’s faithfulness (see also Isaiah 40:10; 50:8).

title: Isaiah 49:6
content_html: a light for the nations: As the Messiah (God's chosen one), the servant's mission would reach the entire world (see Isaiah 11:10, 12; 42:6). The apostle Paul explained his mission to the Gentiles based on this prophecy (Acts 13:47; 26:23).

title: Isaiah 49:7
content_html:
• The promised servant will be disliked and rejected by the nations (see Isaiah 52:14–53:5) and by his own people (see also 50:6–7; 53:8–9).

  • Kings and princes will bow low to show respect and humility to the servant (see also Isaiah 52:15). God will lift him up after he suffers. God is loyal to his servant.

title: Isaiah 49:8
content_html: God acts at the right time to achieve his goals (Acts 1:7; 2 Corinthians 6:2). Israel returned to rebuild the land of Israel after the exile in Babylon. The land was restored, and the city was rebuilt, starting with Cyrus’s decree (Isaiah 44:26–28).

title: Isaiah 49:10–11
content_html:
• Like a shepherd, the Lord in his mercy will guide the returning exiles beside cool waters. He promised to comfort them and meet their needs (see also Isaiah 40:11; Psalm 23:1).

  • roads … highways: The Lord would open up the way of salvation.

title: Isaiah 49:12
content_html: The remnant would come from the north (that is, Babylon and Persia), from the west by sea, and from as far south as Egypt.

title: Isaiah 49:13
content_html: This hymn of praise is in response to the salvation that the servant would make possible.

title: Isaiah 49:14
content_html: The prophet gave reasons to rejoice (Isaiah 49:8–13), but the exiles still thought about the disaster. Babylon invaded, destroyed Jerusalem, and took the people of Judah into exile. There, they felt abandoned and forgotten.

title: Isaiah 49:18
content_html: as jewelry and put them on like a bride: Just as a wedding is festive and public, God's restoration of Israel would be visible to everyone (see also Isaiah 61:10).

title: Isaiah 49:19–21
content_html:
• The population growth would extend beyond Israel to include people from other nations who turn to the Lord (see Isaiah 56:6–8; Revelation 7:9).

  • make room: The increased population would need space far beyond Israel's traditional borders (see also Zechariah 2:4).

title: Isaiah 49:22
content_html: bring your sons … carry your daughters: The powerful rulers of the nations, some of whom had once harmed children in Israel, would help them return to Zion (see also Isaiah 60:3–9). In other parts of Isaiah, he imagined people from foreign nations coming to Zion to worship the Lord (see Isaiah 2:2–4).

title: Isaiah 49:23
content_html:
• you will know that I am the LORD: The surprising role reversal, with kings and queens serving the humble exiles upon their return, will show God's power to do the unimaginable (see also Isaiah 60:16).

  • The godly will face temporary disgrace during the exile in Babylon but would not ultimately be put to shame, as the idolaters and ungodly were.

title: Isaiah 49:24
content_html: be snatched … be delivered: The people considered it nearly impossible that they would be rescued from their oppressors.

title: Isaiah 49:25
content_html: The Lord is like a warrior who fights to protect his children, as promised to Abraham (Genesis 12:3; see also Isaiah 54:15).

title: Isaiah 50:1
content_html:
• God was not responsible for the people's problems; their sin caused their exile (see also Isaiah 59:1–15).

  • Jerusalem, the main city of Judah (Psalm 87:5–6), was destroyed, and the people of Judah went into exile in 586 BC.

title: Isaiah 50:1–3
content_html: This argument shows that God was not forced to abandon Israel. Therefore, he could choose to accept the nation back as his people if he wished.

title: Isaiah 50:2
content_html:
• Why did no one answer: The people did not respond to God's repeated call to trust him and repent of their sins. They were deaf and stubborn (see Isaiah 6:9–10; 29:18; 35:5; 42:18–19).

  • The Lord came and called the people through the prophets (see also Isaiah 65:1–3).

  • do I lack the strength to deliver you?: God could rescue Israel and Judah from the Assyrians and Babylonians, but he first had to address his people's sinfulness (see Isaiah 59:1).

  • My rebuke dries up the sea: This refers to the parting of the Red Sea during the exodus from Egypt (Exodus 14:21–22).

title: Isaiah 50:3
content_html: People connected events like darkness and wearing mourning clothes with the day of the Lord (Isaiah 13:10; see study note on Isaiah 2:5–22). The mention of darkness might refer to the plague of darkness in Egypt (Exodus 10:21).

title: Isaiah 50:4
content_html: The Lord, who never gets tired (Isaiah 40:28), sends the servant with good news (see 40:28–31) to comfort those who are tired (see study note on Isaiah 40:1–31).

title: Isaiah 50:4–11
content_html: This is the third of four servant songs (see study note on Isaiah 42:1–4). The loyal and suffering servant represents an ideal Israel. This image came true in Jesus Christ.

title: Isaiah 50:6
content_html: struck Me … tore out My beard … scorn and spittle: These connections with the experience of Jesus at his crucifixion are too close to not be intentional. Jesus is the true suffering servant (see Matthew 27:27–31).

title: Isaiah 50:7
content_html:
• Externally, the servant would be disgraced and shamed by others. However, inside himself he would wait calmly for God to prove that he was right (see Isaiah 29:22; 54:4; 61:7). He trusted that God would defend him and clear his name.

  • set My face like flint: The servant would be determined to follow God's will (see Luke 9:51).

title: Isaiah 50:8
content_html:
• The One who vindicates Me is near: God defends those who suffer for following his will. Vindication is an act of God where he lifts up those wrongly shamed and punishes the wicked, proud, and arrogant (see also 2 Thessalonians 1:6–9).

  • confront … a case against Me: Trust in God’s vindication removes fear and strengthens faith (see Romans 8:31–34).

title: Isaiah 50:9
content_html:
Who is there to condemn Me?: This question expects the answer, “No one!” The servant is confident that no one can prove him guilty.

This statement points forward to the New Testament teaching about justification (God declaring a person to be right with him). Paul speaks about this in Romans 8:31–35).

title: Isaiah 50:10
content_html:
• To see if someone respects the Lord, observe how they treat God's righteous servant. This idea is similar to the statement Jesus made that no one can reach the Father except through him (John 14:6).

  • Those who trust in the Lord have strong hope for the future (see Isaiah 12:2).

title: Isaiah 50:10–11
content_html: This is a call for response to the servant’s ministry.

title: Isaiah 50:11
content_html: all you who kindle a fire: Spiritually complacent people do not respond to God. They may feel comfortable and secure now, but they will soon experience great suffering (see Isaiah 66:24).

title: Isaiah 51:1
content_html: The rock and the quarry represent Abraham and Sarah (Isaiah 51:2).

title: Isaiah 51:1–8
content_html: This prophecy urges trust in the Lord. Each of its three parts starts with the phrase Listen to Me (Isaiah 51:1, 4, 7).

title: Isaiah 51:2
content_html: Abraham … I blessed him and multiplied him: The exiled community had decreased in number. They needed to have faith that God could restore them again to a healthy and thriving population.

title: Isaiah 51:3
content_html: Eden … the garden of the LORD: God’s salvation will one day reestablish conditions like those in which human beings first lived (see Genesis 2–3).

title: Isaiah 51:4
content_html:
• a law … My justice: The servant's work will succeed because justice will guide God's kingdom on earth (see Isaiah 42:1–4).

  • a light to the nations: The nations will accept the Lord and his revelation (see also Isaiah 42:6; 49:6; 60:1–3; 66:19–23; Matthew 4:15–16; Luke 2:32; John 1:4–9; 12:35–50; Acts 13:47).

title: Isaiah 51:5
content_html: God is strong enough to crush his enemies and rescue his people, such as when he rescued Israel in the exodus (see Isaiah 51:9; Exodus 6:6; 15:16).

title: Isaiah 51:6
content_html: die like gnats: Many wicked people will die on the day of God's judgment.

title: Isaiah 51:7
content_html:
• My law in your hearts: The Lord wants his people to understand, value, and follow his word (see also Psalm 37:31; Jeremiah 31:33).

  • Those who follow the Lord often face ridicule and insults (see also Matthew 5:10–12).

title: Isaiah 51:9–10
content_html:
• Awake, awake: God does not sleep. This prayer asks God to act immediately to save his people (see Isaiah 52:1; see especially God’s military clothing in 59:17).

  • in generations of old: The exodus from Egypt was an act of redemption. It showed God’s power as he made a path of escape (Isaiah 51:10) for his people (see also 11:15).

title: Isaiah 51:13
content_html:
• you have forgotten the LORD: The Lord will never forget Israel (see Isaiah 44:21; 49:15), so they should remember him.

  • The Creator of the world also created the nation of Israel.

title: Isaiah 51:14
content_html: The exile in Babylon brought harsh conditions like imprisonment, starvation, and death.

title: Isaiah 51:15
content_html: I am the LORD: The Lord uses this phrase to show he is the covenant God. It assures his people that his words are true and his actions are reliable (see Isaiah 27:3–4).

title: Isaiah 51:16
content_html:
• My words in your mouth: The focus shifted back to the servant.

  • You are My people: Israel was the covenant community.

title: Isaiah 51:17
content_html:
• Awake, awake!: Israel used these words in a prayer to the Lord (Isaiah 51:9). Israel's problems were not because God was slow to act. Instead, the people were slow to believe God's promises.

  • the cup of His fury: The Lord will give judgment in a just and measured way. He will judge in the right amount and at the right time.

    Those who come under his judgment are described as drinking from a cup of his wrath. This is a picture that shows they must fully experience the results of their sin (see also Matthew 26:39).

title: Isaiah 51:17–23
content_html: The prophet called the people of Israel to be done with their past and to anticipate God’s future for them.

title: Isaiah 51:20
content_html:
Your sons have fainted: This phrase shows how terrible Jerusalem’s destruction was. Even children became weak and collapsed from hunger and suffering.

The book of Lamentations also describes this deep sorrow and pain (Lamentations 1:13, 22; 2:11–12, 19).

title: Isaiah 51:22
content_html: God will defend Israel, just as he did when Assyria attacked Jerusalem (Isaiah 38:6).

title: Isaiah 51:23
content_html: The Babylonians were the ones who caused destruction, war, and famine (Isaiah 51:19).

title: Isaiah 52:1
content_html:
• This second call to wake up (see Isaiah 51:17) was in preparation for a glorious future.

  • holy city: The new city would be holy because its citizens would be holy (see Israel 4:3).

  • will no longer enter you: Things that are unclean will not be permitted to enter the holy city (see Revelation 21:27).

title: Isaiah 52:3
content_html: You were sold for nothing … you will be redeemed: The Lord was in control when he allowed his people to be captured by their enemies. He stayed in control and could save them if he wanted to.

title: Isaiah 52:4
content_html: went down to Egypt to live, then Assyria oppressed them: The exodus from exile would resemble Israel's past exodus from Egypt.

title: Isaiah 52:5
content_html:
• The oppressors would shout with joy because they thought they had defeated both Israel and its God.

  • My name is blasphemed continually all day long: The state of God's people influences God's reputation in the eyes of the world (see Isaiah 37:6, 23; 48:11).

title: Isaiah 52:6
content_html: Just as God revealed himself to Moses before the exodus (Exodus 3:15), he will reveal himself in the future rescue from exile.

title: Isaiah 52:7
content_html:
• How beautiful on the mountains: See Nahum 1:15; Romans 10:15.

  • The good news is more than ending the exile in Babylon. It also points to the peace and salvation of the age to come (Isaiah 54:13–14; see 40:9–10; 41:27).

title: Isaiah 52:7–12
content_html:
This passage uses poetry to describe God’s promise to save his people. Their sin had separated them from God (it had broken their relationship with him). God promised to remove that separation and bring them back to himself.

The picture is of a city surrounded by enemy armies. The people inside the city are waiting for news. They hope to hear that their hero has defeated the enemy and won the battle.

Paul uses these words to speak about the good news of the Lord Jesus Christ (Romans 10:15). He shows that this promise is fulfilled in the message about Jesus.

title: Isaiah 52:8
content_html:
• The watchmen were those seeking news of Zion's redemption.

  • the LORD returns to Zion: The sin of those who lived in the city had driven God away.

title: Isaiah 52:10
content_html: the salvation of our God: God saving his people from shame hints at a greater victory. God will rule triumphantly everywhere (note the similarities with Psalm 98:1–3).

title: Isaiah 52:11
content_html: Here the imagery of leaving the unclean enemy city encourages the people to purify themselves by turning away from all known sin. Those who carry home the sacred objects of the Lord need to be pure.

title: Isaiah 52:11–12
content_html: This is a call to escape the captivity of sin (see Isaiah 48:20 on leaving physical captivity in Babylon). See also 2 Corinthians 6:17; 2 Timothy 2:19.

title: Isaiah 52:12
content_html:
• you will not leave in a hurry: At the time of the exodus, the people had to leave quickly (see Exodus 12:11, 31–36). Isaiah highlighted this difference to emphasize the newness of God's plan.

  • your rear guard: See Exodus 13:21; 14:19–20.

title: Isaiah 52:13
content_html: prosper … highly exalted: The servant will be wise and successful, standing with the great and noble. Similar terms describe God in Isaiah 6:1 and 57:15.

title: Isaiah 52:13–53.12
content_html:
This is the fourth of four passages that speak about the promised servant (see study note on Isaiah 42:1–4). The Hebrew poem is carefully constructed in five three-line stanzas.It begins with the servant’s exaltation (52:13), proceeds to his humiliation (52:14–53:9), and ends with his exaltation (53:10–12).

The faithful servant’s suffering would bring reconciliation between God and humans. The Lord Jesus perfectly fulfilled this prophecy (see Isaiah 40; Matthew 8:17; Acts 8:30–35; Romans 10:15–17; 15:21; 1 Peter 2:24–25).

title: Isaiah 52:14
content_html: many were appalled: No one expected that the rescuer would suffer and die.

title: Isaiah 52:14–15
content_html: Despite the servant’s greatness, the actual way he would appear on earth would surprise people.

title: Isaiah 52:15
content_html:
• sprinkle many nation: They will be amazed that a rescuer appears weak and helpless, leaving them speechless.

  • What they will see and what they will understand will be in complete contrast to anything they had previously imagined.

title: Isaiah 53:1
content_html:
• our message: Scholars have debated who the speaker could be. The main possibilities are:

    1. a faithful remnant of Israel, and

    2. Isaiah himself.

    Most likely, Isaiah the prophet was speaking for and with Israel.

  • the arm: This refers to God's strength, vividly described in the previous chapters (see Isaiah 50:2; 51:5, 9; 52:10). It would show itself in weakness and apparent helplessness through the servant's humiliation and exaltation (see 1 Corinthians 1:27–30).

title: Isaiah 53:2
content_html:
• a tender shoot … out of dry ground: Such a plant is at risk of dying out (compare Isaiah 37:27). It struggles to survive, much less offer anything to others.

  • no stately form or majesty: The servant seemed to lack greatness or obvious royal splendor (see Isaiah 52:13–15).

  • to attract us: People prefer leaders who are good-looking and charismatic. The servant would be neither.

title: Isaiah 53:3
content_html:
• a man of sorrows, acquainted with grief: The servant would suffer deeply. He would know pain and sadness from personal experience.

    He would fully experience the results of human sin and the fall (the time when the first humans disobeyed God and brought suffering into the world).

  • we esteemed Him not: People would not see how someone weak and insignificant could help them, so they would ignore his suffering.

title: Isaiah 53:4
content_html:
• The uncaring world might think the servant caused his own suffering, not realizing he suffered for them.

  • infirmities … sorrows: These descriptions of the servant's humiliation contrast with those of his exaltation.

title: Isaiah 53:5
content_html:
• He was pierced: See also Zechariah 12:10.

  • crushed … punishment … stripes: These were typical punishments for crimes. Sin is a crime against God.

  • brought us peace: The Hebrew word shalom is usually translated as "peace." Shalom means experiencing wholeness in body, mind, and relationships with others. The servant would be injured so humanity can be whole and healthy in all aspects of life (see also Isaiah 57:18). We do not need to suffer divine punishment for our sins because the servant has already done so (see Galatians 1:4).

title: Isaiah 53:6
content_html: gone astray … turned to his own way: These phrases are metaphors for sin (see also Romans 3:10–18).

title: Isaiah 53:7
content_html: See 1 Peter 2:21–25 to see how Jesus Christ fulfills this prophecy.

title: Isaiah 53:7–8
content_html: The Ethiopian eunuch was reading this passage when Philip met him (see Acts 8:32–33).

title: Isaiah 53:8
content_html:
• By oppression and judgment: The servant will not receive legal protection or a proper defense.

  • cut off from the land of the living: To die in midlife was understood to be God’s judgment.

title: Isaiah 53:9
content_html:
• no violence, nor was any deceit: See 1 Peter 2:21–25.

  • with a rich man in His death (see Matthew 27:57–61): Although the Bible often views riches as a blessing from God, it frequently condemns the rich as dishonest and oppressive. The point here might be ironic: This good man would be buried with oppressors.

title: Isaiah 53:10
content_html:
• The servant's grief would lead to a greater good: the forgiveness and reconciliation of sinful humanity. Note also God’s good plan to prosper and exalt the servant.

  • when His soul is made a guilt offering: The servant's suffering served as a substitute for others, similar to animal sacrifices in the Temple.

  • Having many descendants and enjoying a long life are rewards for living wisely and righteously (see Proverbs 3:2; 17:6; 20:7). Because the servant trusted his fate to God, he would receive eternal rewards from God, who vindicates the righteous (see Philippians 2:9–11).

title: Isaiah 53:10–12
content_html: The final stanza of the poem first explains why the servant suffered and was treated unjustly in the place of others; it then explains what the result of that obedience would be.

title: Isaiah 53:11
content_html:
• His knowledge: This does not mean knowledge in the sense of ideas in the mind. It refers to everything the servant would live through. It includes his obedience, his suffering, and his close relationship with God.

  • Because the servant obeys God fully, many people can be put right with God. This is called justification (God declaring a person to be right with him). He can do this because he will carry and bear their sins (see Genesis 15:6; Romans 5:18–19).

title: Isaiah 53:12
content_html:
• a portion: See Philippians 2:9–11.

  • He was numbered with the transgressors: Transgressors (Hebrew poshe‘im) is a stronger word than sinners and is important in Isaiah. It refers to those who defy lawful authority, in this case, God.

  • intercession for the transgressors: The servant would pray for sinners while suffering (see Exodus 32:30; Luke 23:34).

title: Isaiah 54:1
content_html: In the ancient world, a woman who had not given birth after marriage would feel ashamed. Isaiah compares Jerusalem to a woman without children who celebrates when she finally has them (see Galatians 4:27).

title: Isaiah 54:1–3
content_html: The prophet encourages Jerusalem, likened to a barren woman, to rejoice because its fate was changing very quickly. She would have so many children that she would outgrow her home (see Galatians 4:27).

title: Isaiah 54:1–17
content_html: Salvation flows from the vindication of the suffering servant. The promises mentioned here go beyond the return from Babylonian exile and apply to the coming of Jesus Christ, the extension of the kingdom to the church, the benefits of the second coming of Jesus Christ as the bridegroom of the church, and the new Jerusalem.

title: Isaiah 54:1–55.13
content_html: This is an invitation to participate in the restoration to God’s favor made possible through the ministry of the promised servant.

title: Isaiah 54:3
content_html:
• The descendants of Israel (compare Isaiah 53:10) would take over other nations, fulfilling God's promise to Abraham (Genesis 22:17) and Jacob (Genesis 28:14).

  • As desolation occurred (Isaiah 6:11; 14:21), God's people would inherit the earth and rebuild the ruined cities (51:6; 52:10; 65:17; see also Matthew 5:5; Romans 4:13).

title: Isaiah 54:5
content_html:
• husband … Maker: The Lord committed to the abandoned woman (his people in exile) as her maker (Isaiah 44:24) and husband (see also Isaiah 62:4–5; Hosea 2:19–20).

  • The Redeemer transforms misery into freedom and fulfillment (see also Isaiah 41:14).

title: Isaiah 54:7–8
content_html:
• The pain of separation would be brief compared to the depth of the renewal of love and compassion.

  • The Lord abandoned Israel in exile for a little while because of the people’s sins.

  • everlasting kindness: The Lord is always committed to his people, making the 70-year exile seem brief (see Isaiah 25:1; Hosea 2:1).

title: Isaiah 54:9
content_html: The exile was similar in both drama and trauma to the flood in the time of Noah. In both instances, people sinned grievously against the Lord, but the Lord renewed his commitment to creation (after Noah’s flood) and to his people (after the exile).

title: Isaiah 54:10
content_html:
• God's faithful love for his people continues even when they are unfaithful.

  • God's covenant of blessing assures his presence, bringing wholeness, blessing, and protection. It replaces the shame and disgrace of the exile in Babylon (see Ezekiel 34:25; 37:26).

title: Isaiah 54:11
content_html:
• The Lord himself would rebuild the city (see Hebrews 11:10).

  • Sapphires are a semi-precious stone.

title: Isaiah 54:11–17
content_html: This describes a vision of Jerusalem renewed as a city under God's protection, a place of peace and righteousness (see Isaiah 59:21–60:22). This section provides the background for John's vision of the new Jerusalem (Revelation 21:10–21).

title: Isaiah 54:13
content_html:
• will be taught: Jesus alluded to this verse in John 6:45.

  • They will experience great peace, along with the benefits of God's presence and protection (Isaiah 53:5; see 48:17–18; 54:10).

title: Isaiah 54:14
content_html: Jerusalem would again be a righteous city, secure under a government that is just and fair (see Isaiah 1:26).

title: Isaiah 54:15
content_html: God assures that no nation can defeat his people. The Lord promises to protect them, just as he promised Abraham (Genesis 12:3; see Isaiah 49:25).

title: Isaiah 54:17
content_html:
• No weapon … shall prosper … accuses … vindication: God will protect his people in war and in the courtroom.

  • This is the heritage of the servants of the LORD: Only the true children of God, from Israel and other nations, will enjoy the promised blessings of the future age that God establishes.

title: Isaiah 55:1
content_html:
• thirsty … wine and milk: The invitation to eat and drink is like Wisdom’s call in Proverbs 9:5. This image promises to quench thirst not only with water but also with more expensive items like wine and milk.

    John applied a similar theme to Jesus Christ (John 4:14; 6:27, 35; 7:37; Revelation 21:6; 22:17).

  • without cost: Reconciliation with God is for anyone and has no cost.

title: Isaiah 55:1–13
content_html:
This final chapter of the prophecies of comfort (Isaiah 40–55) summarizes the section’s main themes:

  • Blessing

  • Covenant

  • Witness

  • Word

  • Nations

  • Glory

  • Forgiveness

  • Joy

title: Isaiah 55:2
content_html: Listen carefully to Me, and eat what is good: Responding positively to God satisfies one’s spiritual, social, and physical being (see 1:19; 58:13–14; Proverbs 4:10).

title: Isaiah 55:3
content_html:
• come to Me; listen, so that your soul may live: Those who obey God's word find eternal life (see also Isaiah 55:11).

  • an everlasting covenant … promised to David: God gave King David a special promise to preserve his royal line (see 2 Samuel 7:15–16). God promised that David’s family line would continue forever. This promise is fulfilled in the kingship of the Messiah (God’s chosen king), Jesus Christ (see Isaiah 9:6–7; 11:1–16; Acts 2:22–36; 13:34).

title: Isaiah 55:6
content_html: while He is near: When God kindly offers an invitation to salvation, people must respond (Isaiah 65:1; Jeremiah 29:13–14; Hosea 5:6; 10:12). Those who do not seek him during these times may never have another chance.

title: Isaiah 55:6–9
content_html: The prophet calls for a response while the time is right.

title: Isaiah 55:7
content_html:
• Let the wicked man forsake his way and … return to the LORD: True conversion means changing how we live to follow God's rules (see Isaiah 1:16–17; 30:15; 59:20).

  • have compassion: God's compassion reaches those in need, wherever they are.

  • He will freely pardon: Forgiveness of sin is key to the good news of redemption (see Ephesians 1:7; Colossians 1:14; see also Psalms 32:1; 86:5; 99:8; 103:2–5).

title: Isaiah 55:8–9
content_html: My thoughts are not your thoughts: God's plans are wonderful (Psalm 92:5; compare Psalm 94:11). God's creations, including humans, cannot fully understand the Creator's thoughts. However, his messengers reveal insights and knowledge about some of his actions.

title: Isaiah 55:10–11
content_html: The Creator sends rain, snow, and his word (Hebrew dabar). Here, dabar means his will or plan (see Isaiah 14:24; 40:8). God's written word expresses his plan and achieves his goals. God is successful in all his actions (see Isaiah 14:26–27; 46:10).

title: Isaiah 55:12
content_html:
• Joy is found in being redeemed from bondage.

  • Godly people experience an inner peace not known to the wicked (Isaiah 48:22).

  • mountains and hills … trees of the field: Creation participates in the freedom of the children of God (Isaiah 44:23; 49:11, 13; see Romans 8:19–23).

title: Isaiah 55:12–13
content_html:
The prophet tells the people not to focus on the past. He urges them to leave Babylon and return home.

He also calls them to see things from the viewpoint of the coming age (the new time of salvation that God is about to bring).

title: Isaiah 55:13
content_html:
• Redemption is like the transformation from desert to forest (see also Isaiah 35:1; 41:19; 60:13).

  • this will make a name for the LORD: God's glory will become increasingly visible to people as his redemption takes effect.

title: Isaiah 56:1
content_html:
• Maintain justice and do what is right: Isaiah 1–39 emphasize the importance of justice in relationships with others and God. True godliness comes from having a character shaped by God's character, achieved by understanding and consistently applying God's word.

  • My salvation is coming soon: This summarizes the message of Isaiah 40–55, where the Lord is creating a world of harmony, peace, restoration, vindication, and the removal of enemies (Isaiah 46:13).

title: Isaiah 56:1–8
content_html: This section summarizes chapters 1–55 with an invitation to the outcasts to participate in God’s redemption.

title: Isaiah 56:1–66.24
content_html: This last major division of the book of Isaiah brings together themes from chapters 1–39 (sin, justice, righteousness, responsibility, vengeance, and vindication) and chapters 40–55 (salvation and the age to come).

title: Isaiah 56:2
content_html:
• Blessed: Compare Isaiah 30:18; 32:20; see also Psalms 1:1; 119:1; Matthew 5:3.

  • The Sabbath is a sign of the covenant (Exodus 31:13–17), and is God's gift to his people. It applies to both the present age and the age to come (Isaiah 56:4, 6; 58:13–14; see Hebrews 4:1–13).

title: Isaiah 56:3
content_html:
• Foreigners who commit themselves to the Lord would become full members of his covenant community. In earlier times, their participation had strict limits (Deuteronomy 23:3, 7–8).

  • eunuch: In the past, a man who had been emasculated was not allowed to take part fully in the community (Deuteronomy 23:1). Both eunuchs and foreigners were seen as outside Israel’s holiness. They were considered on the edge of God’s people.

    But in the age to come, God would bring those on the margins to the center. Those who were once excluded would be welcomed and honored (Matthew 20:16).

title: Isaiah 56:3–7
content_html: The blessing (56:2) would extend to eunuchs and to the Gentiles.

title: Isaiah 56:4
content_html: The Sabbath is a sign of the covenant (Exodus 31:13–17). It shows the special relationship between the Lord and Israel.

title: Isaiah 56:5
content_html:
• in My house and within My walls: Those once excluded will have a place with God.

  • a memorial and a name: Everlasting existence in God’s presence is better than the blessing of physical descendants.

title: Isaiah 56:6
content_html:
• The essential thing required for covenant fellowship is love for God (Deuteronomy 6:5; 30:20; Matthew 22:34–38).

  • Keeping the Sabbath is a sign of keeping the covenant itself (see Exodus 31:13–17).

title: Isaiah 56:7
content_html:
• burnt offerings and sacrifices: In the past, God rejected Israel’s offerings when the people were living sinful lives (Isaiah 1:11–13). Their worship did not please him because their hearts were not right.

    But God would accept offerings from righteous gentiles, that is, people from other nations who truly follow him. Being part of a certain nation does not matter if a person does not truly honor God.

  • a house of prayer for all the nations: The Lord would open the temple to all nations (see Isaiah 2:2–4). Jesus criticized the people for disrespecting the temple and preventing it from being a house of prayer (Matthew 21:13; Mark 11:17).

title: Isaiah 56:8
content_html:
• The outcasts of Israel were people scattered among other nations due to the exile in Babylon (see also Isaiah 11:12).

  • The others included eunuchs and non-Jews (Isaiah 56:3–7; see also 57:19; John 10:16).

title: Isaiah 56:9
content_html: Wild animals are here a metaphor for the hostile nations surrounding Judah.

title: Isaiah 56:9–57.13
content_html: This passage reflects on how common evil had become among God’s people. Many of them were greedy and wanted more and more for themselves (Isaiah 56:9–12). They treated righteous people with hatred and violence (Isaiah 57:1–2). They also worshiped idols instead of the true God (Isaiah 57:3–13).

title: Isaiah 56:10
content_html:
• The leaders of Israel failed to guide and protect God’s people from the sins that led to the exile in Babylon.

  • dreamers … loving to slumber: See Isaiah 29:10.

title: Isaiah 56:11
content_html:
• shepherds with no discernment: Israel's leaders did not know how to rule in a godly way (see also Ezekiel 34:1–6). In contrast, the Lord is the faithful shepherd of his people (Isaiah 40:11).

  • they all turn to their own way: They did not care about God or his standards for leadership (see also Isaiah 53:6).

  • his own gain: Compare Isaiah 33:15.

title: Isaiah 57:1
content_html:
• the presence of evil: Judgment that would come on the wicked nations.

  • taken away: At times, God allows the godly to die in order to protect them from harsher times to come.

title: Isaiah 57:1–2
content_html: This is a lament for the righteous who suffer in a wicked society.

title: Isaiah 57:2
content_html: The wicked would not be able to rest in peace (57:21).

title: Isaiah 57:3
content_html: sons of a sorceress … offspring of adulterers and prostitutes: Baal religion, which flourished in Israel before the exile (see Isaiah 1:21–23), included immoral sexual practices.

title: Isaiah 57:4
content_html: mocking … stick out your tongue: The wicked oppressed the righteous by their insults (Isaiah 57:1–2; see also 28:9, 14).

title: Isaiah 57:5
content_html:
• among the oaks, under every luxuriant tree: People often used groups of trees as places to worship false gods. These tree groves were common sites for pagan worship (see Isaiah 1:29–30).

  • slaughter your children: Some people in Israel practiced the terrible act of child sacrifice. They offered their children to false gods. This happened at certain times in Israel’s history (see Psalm 106:37–38).

title: Isaiah 57:6
content_html: People believed that liquid offerings and grain offerings would meet the basic needs of their gods. They thought these gifts would please the gods and cause them to show favor.

title: Isaiah 57:8
content_html:
uncovered your bed: This phrase refers to sexual sin connected with idol worship. The people of Israel were deeply committed to fertility religions. These religions included sexual acts as part of their worship ceremonies.

This behavior showed how far they had turned away from the Lord.

title: Isaiah 57:11
content_html: The people were living in fear of false gods instead of fearing the Lord (see Isaiah 8:12–13).

title: Isaiah 57:12
content_html: The Lord did not see the people's righteousness (Isaiah 59:16) as truly righteous.

title: Isaiah 57:13
content_html:
• Your companies of idols … take them away: See study note on Isaiah 40:18.

  • inherit the land: The Hebrew word translated land can also refer to the whole earth (see Psalms 25:13; 37:9, 11, 22, 29, 34; Matthew 5:5).

  • My holy mountain: God's servants will enjoy his presence in Zion (see Isaiah 2:2; 56:7).

title: Isaiah 57:14–21
content_html: The Lord promises to be with the humble, heal them, and give them peace. This promise is for all citizens of Zion, both Jews and non-Jews. The holy one offers salvation to the humble and deals fairly with the wicked.

title: Isaiah 57:15
content_html: God is high and lifted up, yet he promises to live with the humble.

title: Isaiah 57:18
content_html: but I will heal him: Salvation is by God’s grace (see Isaiah 19:22; 30:26; 53:5).

title: Isaiah 57:19
content_html:
• praise to their lips: Those who experience God's redemption should respond with praise.

  • those far and near: This includes both Gentiles and Jews (see Isaiah 56:3; John 10:16; Ephesians 2:17).

title: Isaiah 57:20
content_html:
• storm-tossed sea … it cannot be still: People who constantly oppose God (see Psalms 46:3, 6; 93:3–4) do not find inner peace because they are not at peace with God (Isaiah 30:15; 32:17).

  • churn up mire and muck: Their actions are often unproductive (see Psalm 40:2).

title: Isaiah 57:21
content_html: no peace: Compare Isaiah 57:2; see also 48:22.

title: Isaiah 58:1–4
content_html: This is a charge against religious Israelites who were pleased with themselves because of their religious activities.

title: Isaiah 58:2
content_html: they seek me: They may have been truly committed to a religious form, but they failed to truly meet God in their religious activities.

title: Isaiah 58:3
content_html: Fasting should be a time of self-deprivation in order to focus on God and pleasing him (Leviticus 16:29; see Zechariah 7:5). However, these people were only interested in pleasing themselves and continuing in their sins.

title: Isaiah 58:5
content_html:
• This passage uses strong language to show the problem with the people’s fasting. Their fasting was only outward religious behavior. They did not truly repent or do what is right.

    Fasting without sincere hearts and righteous actions is empty. It is only for show.

  • People wore sackcloth and put ashes on their heads to show sorrow and mourning.

  • acceptable to the LORD?: The people wrongly believed that God would bless them if they performed the correct rituals. They thought the actions themselves would bring blessing.

title: Isaiah 58:6–7
content_html: True fasting makes people aware of injustice and oppression. It encourages them to help those in need (see Isaiah 42:7; Matthew 25:35–36; James 1:27).

title: Isaiah 58:8
content_html:
• your light: The light of the age to come will shine on those who fully turn to the Lord (see Isaiah 42:6–7; 60:1–3). The time of divine judgment and oppression will end.

  • your healing: The nation of Israel was wounded when they were conquered and sent into exile.

  • your rear guard: See also Isaiah 52:12.
